AI.motivation: The motivation is to build upon Maurice Pagnucco's AGM-like abduction framework by addressing the challenge of incorporating contradictory hypotheses without trivializing the epistemic state or resulting in inconsistency.
AI.method: The method involves defining postulates and a partial meet construction for the paraconsistent abductive expansion using the paraconsistent logic RCbr, a formal logic specifically suited for handling inconsistencies.
AI.result: The paper develops a paraconsistent AGM-like abductive expansion operation, termed as AGMpabd, which integrates contradictory hypotheses without trivializing belief systems.
AI.conclusion: The creation of AGMpabd demonstrates a novel approach in the AGM literature for managing abductive expansions under contradictions, although it does not yet fully leverage paraconsistent operators. Further exploration is defined for a second paper, AGMcircabd, which will aim to address this limitation.

title: Manifold Constrained Tabular Deep Neural Networks
summary: Tabular classification is often governed by local, condition-triggered rules rather than smooth global patterns. However, tabular deep neural networks (DNNs) are typically built upon Euclidean representations that favor smooth variations and semantic locality. This potential geometric mismatch can make it challenging for tabular DNNs to efficiently represent the discrete, rule-partitioned structures often underlying tabular classification. To address this issue, we propose HDE-Net, a manifold-constrained DNN that enables hierarchical decision modeling in hyperbolic space. We first abstract heterogeneous features into unified Latent Decision Nodes (LDNs) and embed them in the Poincaré ball, forming a continuous representation that resembles tree-structured reasoning. For numerical features, we introduce a Soft Decision Routing mechanism that approximates range-based local rules in a differentiable manner, bringing their LDN semantics closer to those of categorical features. An entropy-aware capacity allocation algorithm further adapts the number of LDNs per numerical feature to balance expressiveness and complexity. On the TALENT-tiny-core classification benchmark (30 datasets), HDE-Net achieves the \textit{best average rank}, outperforming both industrial GBDTs and recent tabular DNNs while maintaining high efficiency.

AI.tldr: The authors introduce HDE-Net, a deep neural network utilizing hyperbolic space for tabular classification, achieving superior performance through hierarchical decision modeling.
AI.motivation: Traditional tabular DNNs struggle with matching the discrete rule-partitioned nature of tabular classification due to their reliance on Euclidean representations.
AI.method: HDE-Net employs manifold-constrained learning in hyperbolic space, utilizing Latent Decision Nodes (LDNs), Soft Decision Routing, and entropy-aware capacity allocation for efficient modeling.
AI.result: HDE-Net outperforms industrial GBDTs and recent tabular DNNs on 30 datasets in the TALENT-tiny-core benchmark, achieving the best average rank while being efficient.
AI.conclusion: Using hyperbolic space and hierarchical decision modeling delivers better representation for tabular data classification, addressing geometric mismatches and improving accuracy.

title: Low-Rank Attention Residuals
summary: Attention Residuals replace the fixed residual sum with depthwise attention over previous sub-layer outputs in large language models (LLMs), but use each output as both a full-dimensional key and value. This couples routing with representation and makes depth-routing scores scale with the hidden width $d$. We propose Low-Rank Attention Residuals (LR-AttnRes), which keep full-dimensional residual values while using $r$-dimensional keys, with $r \ll d$, for routing. Projected LR-AttnRes emits learned low-rank keys from existing output projections, decoupling routing from residual content and achieving the best validation loss among the variants tested. Sliced LR-AttnRes uses the last $r$ dimensions of each value as the routing key, removing the auxiliary key-projection path and reducing residual-side FLOPs while still improving performance. Comprehensive sweeps show that depthwise routing can be effective with far fewer dimensions than the model width. We release code and models to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Low-Rank Attention Residuals (LR-AttnRes) to improve depthwise attention in large language models by decoupling routing from residual representations using low-dimensional keys.
AI.motivation: Address limitations in existing attention residuals where coupling of routing and representation affects scaling with model width.
AI.method: Introduce LR-AttnRes architectures using low-dimensional keys for routing while keeping residual values full-dimensional. Two approaches—Projected LR-AttnRes and Sliced LR-AttnRes—reduce computational complexity and improve performance.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show that depthwise routing with low-dimensional keys outperforms existing methods and reduces computation costs. Projected LR-AttnRes achieves the best validation loss.
AI.conclusion: LR-AttnRes offers an effective way to enhance attention residuals with fewer dimensions, improving both performance and efficiency in large-scale language models. Code and models are made available for further research.

title: A Biomimetic Myoelectric Tentacle Prosthesis with Sensorless Object Detection and Vibrotactile Feedback
summary: This paper presents the design and evaluation of a myoelectric tentacle-shaped prosthesis integrating electromyographic (EMG) control, sensorless object detection, and vibrotactile feedback. The objective was to develop a responsive and intuitive assistive device that adapts to various object shapes while providing sensory feedback to the user. The system relies on EMG signals to control the motion of a flexible, biomimetic structure whose curling geometry follows a logarithmic spiral, enabling it to coil around objects. To ensure stable control, the EMG signal is normalized and filtered, and a threshold-based method identifies user intention. Object contact is detected through a slope-based analysis of motor current, eliminating the need for external sensors, and a haptic feedback strategy based on cumulative vibrotactile stimulation conveys spatial information about the tentacle's configuration. The system was evaluated through quantitative and qualitative tests. The results demonstrate a low response time (77 ms on average), enabling smooth real-time interaction; an object-detection success rate above 90%, confirming robustness despite EMG variability; and an effective haptic feedback strategy that allowed users to reliably identify the folding zone of the tentacle. The proposed biomimetic design promotes further investigation of expressive artificial limbs by prioritizing expressive functionality over adherence to a predefined, anthropomorphic form factor.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a tentacle-shaped prosthesis with EMG control, sensorless object detection, and vibrotactile feedback that enables intuitive, adaptive interaction with objects.
AI.motivation: To create a responsive, intuitive prosthesis that adapts to various object shapes while providing users with effective sensory feedback.
AI.method: The system uses EMG signals for control, a logarithmic spiral geometry for object grasping, threshold and slope-based methods for intention and object detection, and vibrotactile feedback to convey spatial information.
AI.result: The evaluation showed an average response time of 77 ms, an object-detection success rate above 90%, and effective haptic feedback enabling users to identify tentacle zones reliably.
AI.conclusion: The biomimetic tentacle prosthesis demonstrates the potential of prioritizing expressive functionality over anthropomorphic design, offering robust, intuitive assistive technology for users.

title: Lifelong Representations: A Survey on Continual Self-Supervised Learning for Vision Models
comment: This work has been accepted for publication in IEEE EAIS 2026
summary: Traditionally, continual learning has assumed access to labeled data, yet many real-world applications -- such as lifelong robotics -- require models to adapt continuously from unlabeled streams. This has led to the development of continual self-supervised learning (CSSL), a rapidly growing area that lacks a dedicated, systematic review. In this work, we present a comprehensive survey of CSSL for vision, with connections to emerging vision-language settings. First, we analyze existing evaluation protocols and highlight inconsistencies that hinder fair comparison. We then examine why self-supervised objectives exhibit improved robustness to catastrophic forgetting, relating this to task-agnostic representations and smoother loss landscapes. Next, we organize existing methods into a unified taxonomy based on their forgetting-mitigation strategies, including distillation, replay, regularization, architectural approaches, model merging, and objective-level adaptation. Finally, we identify open challenges such as scalability and the need for fast adaptability. We argue that advancing CSSL requires moving beyond small-scale benchmarks towards continual pre-training paradigms for large-scale systems.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys the field of continual self-supervised learning (CSSL) in vision, analyzing evaluation inconsistencies, robustness mechanisms, method classification, and challenges.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of systematic reviews in the rapidly growing field of CSSL, which is critical for applications like lifelong robotics adapting to unlabeled streams.
AI.method: The authors analyze evaluation protocols, explore robustness mechanisms in self-supervised objectives, create a taxonomy of CSSL methods based on mitigation strategies, and discuss challenges such as scalability and adaptability.
AI.result: The paper identifies inconsistencies in evaluation methods, explains the robustness of self-supervised objectives, categorizes existing CSSL methods systematically, and highlights key challenges.
AI.conclusion: Advancing CSSL requires scalable benchmarks and large-scale pre-training paradigms to enable practical applications involving continuous adaptation.

title: Kinetic Inductors Enable Reversible Logic
comment: 17 pages, 12 figures, submitted for peer review
summary: Reversible logic has long promised substantial reductions in energy dissipation, yet prior demonstrations have not scaled to commercially relevant systems. This work presents a quantitative framework for evaluating reversible logic through a process termed CMOS conversion, in which a conventional CMOS design is transformed into a functionally equivalent reversible implementation and compared using common performance metrics. The framework combines planning equations, kinetic-inductor energy-storage models, a four-phase 4LC energy-recycling power supply, and RLC-based simulation methods that account for data-dependent loading effects. The analysis identifies inductor loss as a fundamental limitation of conventional approaches and shows that high-energy-density kinetic inductors provide essential design margin for scaling reversible systems. Using representative device parameters, the framework suggests that selected cryogenic CMOS qubit controller circuits could be converted to reversible logic using available or near-term technologies. Rather than claiming commercialization of reversible logic in general, the paper provides a methodology for assessing its feasibility and potential benefits across future applications.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework for evaluating the feasibility and benefits of reversible logic through CMOS conversion and identifies limitations and potential for scalable systems.
AI.motivation: Reversible logic promises energy dissipation reduction, but prior demonstrations haven't scaled to practical, commercially relevant systems.
AI.method: The authors introduce a CMOS conversion framework that includes planning equations, kinetic-inductor energy-storage models, a four-phase 4LC power supply, and RLC-based simulations to evaluate reversible logic performance.
AI.result: The analysis finds inductor loss to be a key limitation, but high-energy-density kinetic inductors enable scaling. Selected cryogenic CMOS systems could adopt reversible logic with current or near-term technology.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a methodology to assess the feasibility and advantages of reversible logic rather than suggesting immediate commercialization, focusing on future application potentials.

title: Proceedings of the 21st Workshop on Logical Frameworks and Meta Languages: Theory and Practice
summary: This volume of the Electronic Proceedings in Theoretical Computer Science (EPTCS) includes the contributed papers presented at the 21st International Workshop on Logical Frameworks and Meta-Languages: Theory and Practice (LFMTP 2026), in Lisbon, Portugal, on July 24th, 2026, at the Federated Logic Conference (FLoC 2026) as a satellite event of the 11th International Conference on Formal Structures for Computation and Deduction (FSCD 2026). The program committee for this edition of LFMTP was chaired by Olivier Hermant and Sophie Tourret. More information about LFMTP can be found on https://lfmtp.org.

AI.tldr: This document includes papers from the 21st International Workshop on Logical Frameworks and Meta-Languages (LFMTP 2026), held as part of FLoC 2026 in Lisbon.
AI.motivation: The workshop aims to advance theoretical and practical research on logical frameworks and meta-languages, fostering collaboration in these fields.
AI.method: Papers were selected and presented by researchers during the workshop, organized by the program committee led by Olivier Hermant and Sophie Tourret.
AI.result: The volume compiles all contributed papers accepted and presented at the workshop, covering recent advancements and topics in logical frameworks.
AI.conclusion: The event served as a significant platform for sharing knowledge and promoting research in logical frameworks and meta-languages.

title: Model-Driven Digital Twin Framework for Quantum Networks
summary: Quantum networks are advancing towards larger and more operational infrastructures, yet their evaluation remains fragmented across heterogeneous physical platforms, simulators, protocols, and architectural abstractions. Current digital-twin studies for quantum networks mainly realise isolated capabilities or application-specific solutions rather than reusable system-level twins. This paper argues that Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) can provide a systematic basis for integrating and evolving these heterogeneous artefacts. It derives requirements for design-time evaluation and runtime synchronisation, and proposes a progression of architectures from code-driven and domain-model-driven solutions to point-to-point and hub-and-spoke integration. A conceptual implementation case study illustrates this using SysML v2, QKD kit, an EMF-based controller, and SeQUeNCe. The work provides a foundation for adaptable and interoperable digital twins for quantum networks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to systematize quantum networks, addressing evaluation gaps across diverse platforms with a focus on interoperable digital twins.
AI.motivation: The heterogeneous nature of quantum networks and fragmented evaluation methods necessitate a unified framework for systematic integration and evolution of various artefacts.
AI.method: The authors propose a progression of architectures utilizing model-driven engineering principles, supported by a conceptual implementation case study involving tools such as SysML v2, QKD kit, and SeQUeNCe.
AI.result: A structured framework using MDE is shown to enable more adaptable and interoperable digital twins for quantum networks.
AI.conclusion: The research lays the groundwork for creating reusable and efficient digital twins in quantum networks, potentially accelerating their development and standardization.

title: Coresets Before Score Sets: Evaluation-Unsupervised Prompt Subset Selection for LLM Benchmarks
summary: We study LLM benchmark coreset selection: selecting a small subset of prompts over multiple benchmarks whose induced model scores and rankings approximate those obtained from the full benchmark suite. In evaluation-unsupervised benchmark coreset selection (our approach), the selection algorithm uses no model evaluation outcomes, and operates on a fine granularity by producing subsets of prompts over multiple benchmarks rather than producing a sub-collection of entire benchmarks. We use submodular subset selection, and we develop and evaluate many different submodular functions for this purpose, including determinantal point process (DPP) based approaches, submodular mutual information functions, and facility location-based functions. On a new large-scale suite of 35 heterogeneous benchmarks spanning five different capability categories, 18 frontier LLMs, and over 61K prompts, we find that the facility location (FL) function operating exclusively on inexpensive semantic prompt embeddings preserves LLM scores better than twelve separate score-based and diversity-based baselines, across a range of coreset budgets. Moreover, we show our proposed objective is not limited to the evaluation-unsupervised regime: in the setting where only a handful of whole benchmarks must be selected and a large amount of model scores are available, the same objective matches or outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the MMLU and MTEB leaderboards, while being substantially cheaper to compute. Together, our results suggest that submodularity, in general, is a strong and reliable tool for benchmark compression.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the problem of selecting a small subset of prompts from large benchmark suites for LLM evaluation by focusing on maintaining performance accuracy. It uses submodular subset selection methods, achieving strong results with computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper is to design efficient methods for compressing benchmark evaluation tasks while maintaining accuracy, as evaluating large-scale language models on extensive benchmarks is resource-intensive.
AI.method: The authors employ submodular subset selection techniques, including DPP-based, mutual information, and facility location-based functions, to find compact subsets of prompts that approximate full benchmark results without requiring model evaluation outcomes.
AI.result: The facility location function based on semantic prompt embeddings outperforms other score-based and diversity-based baselines in preserving LLM scores across budgets. This approach also excels in settings where some model scores are available, matching or exceeding state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Submodularity-based methods, particularly the facility location function, are highly effective and computationally efficient for benchmark compression, making them strong candidates for future LLM evaluation tasks.

title: Estimation, Prediction, and Assortment Optimization for Markov Chain Choice Models with Panel Data
summary: We propose a framework for the Markov chain (MC) choice model with panel data, including parameter estimation, personalized choice prediction, and personalized assortment optimization. In contrast to the traditional setting, which assumes that each transaction is independently drawn from a random utility model, our framework accounts for dependencies among transactions for the same customer in historical data, captured by partial-ordering preference information. To the best of our knowledge, our framework initiates the study of choice modeling with panel data under MC. As our primary result, we propose novel expectation-maximization (EM) algorithms for MC parameter estimation by incorporating partial-ordering-based customer preference information. On synthetic datasets and the sushi dataset, our EM algorithms outperform the traditional EM algorithm of Simsek and Topaloglu (Operations Research, 66, 2018) and multinomial-logit-based partial-order benchmarks adapted from Jagabathula and Vulcano (Management Science, 64, 2018). As our secondary contribution, we present hardness and computational results for conditional choice prediction and assortment optimization problems. These results complement our estimation framework and clarify the computational landscape of conditional choice and assortment optimization, which may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Markov chain-based choice model for panel data, including new estimation and optimization methods, and shows improved outcomes compared to traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional choice modeling assumes independent transactions, which overlook dependencies in sequential customer behavior. This paper addresses that gap by leveraging panel data and partial-ordering preference information to better model customer choices.
AI.method: The authors develop expectation-maximization (EM) algorithms tailored to Markov chain parameter estimation using partial-ordering preference data. They evaluate these algorithms on both synthetic and real datasets, and also analyze the computational complexity of related problems.
AI.result: Empirical assessments show that the new EM algorithms outperform existing methods in estimating preferences and predicting choices. Additionally, computational analyses on prediction and assortment optimization problems complement the framework.
AI.conclusion: The study advances the field of choice modeling by incorporating panel data with dependent transactions and extending analytical understanding, offering practical and computationally viable tools for personalized recommendation and optimization.

title: Equal Accuracy, Unequal Evidence: Search APIs as Decision Surfaces for Tool-Using Agents
summary: Search APIs are the fundamental retrieval layer for many agents and are often their most frequently used tool. Traditional search APIs provide URLs, titles, and snippets that preview website contents. Because full-page retrieval is token-intensive, agent retrieval architectures increasingly use progressive disclosure: the agent first sees snippets and then chooses whether to fetch full pages. In such systems, search API performance is often evaluated primarily by answer accuracy. We argue that a commercial search API is better understood as a decision surface: the ranked snippets, URLs, and metadata that determine whether an agent answers immediately, searches again, or spends tokens opening pages. We test this claim with one frozen GPT-5.4 agent, two tools (search_web and fetch_page), and 100 questions from SEALQA-HARD, varying only the search provider (Brave, Tavily, Firecrawl). A Kimi-K2.6 oracle labels every content element visible to the agent (URL, title, snippet, and fetched page, when fetched), producing 6,869 valid per-URL judgments. We use an audited correct-answer label, semantic match, which preserves exact matches while accepting harmless formatting and naming variants. Under this measure, the providers remain close (25, 25, 26 / 100), but their evidence economies differ sharply: Brave offers gold-answer-rich snippets, Tavily concentrates gold-supporting URLs at rank 1, and Firecrawl is associated with broader exploration under this fixed agent policy. We also introduce a surface contradiction-to-gold URL ratio, which varies from 0.92 to 2.59. Provider choice is therefore a retrieval-budget and policy decision, not merely a recall decision.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates search API performance as a decision-making surface for agents, focusing on evidence economy and decision impacts rather than just answer accuracy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to better understand the role of commercial search APIs in agent decision-making, beyond the traditional metrics of accuracy, by analyzing their impact on token economy and retrieval strategies.
AI.method: The study uses a frozen GPT-5.4 agent integrated with two tools (search_web and fetch_page) and tests responses to 100 questions from SEALQA-HARD using three search providers (Brave, Tavily, Firecrawl). Data is labeled and analyzed through an oracle system, Kimi-K2.6.
AI.result: The three providers exhibit similar performance in direct answer accuracy but differ sharply in evidence economy: Brave favors rich snippet answers, Tavily excels at top-ranked URL answers, and Firecrawl promotes broader exploration. A novel contradiction-to-gold URL ratio metric highlights substantial differences in decision-making implications between providers.
AI.conclusion: Search provider selection is a crucial budget and policy choice for retrieval architectures, emphasizing the importance of tailored evaluation metrics for API performance.

title: FedCausal-Dyn: A Causal-Dynamic Paradigm for Federated Learning under Dynamic Feature Drift
summary: This paper addresses the challenging problem of dynamic feature drift in federated learning, where data distributions evolve across clients and over time -- a common scenario in real-world applications like financial technology. Existing approaches often assume static drift, limiting their effectiveness in non-stationary environments. To overcome this, we propose \textbf{FedCausal-Dyn}, a novel federated learning framework built on a causal-dynamic paradigm. Its key innovation is \textit{causal-domain feature separation}, which disentangles domain-invariant causal features from spurious, domain-specific variations via specialized projection heads and adversarial training. This enables \textit{reliable and dynamic prototype aggregation}, weighting local class prototypes by estimated reliability before global aggregation. We further introduce \textit{causal-feature guided collaborative regularization}, unifying prototype contrastive alignment and domain invariance into a cohesive objective. Extensive experiments on three federated domain generalization benchmarks demonstrate that FedCausal-Dyn consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, with the highest average accuracy and the most stable results. Ablation studies confirm each component's critical contribution. Our work provides a robust and principled solution for federated learning under dynamic feature drift.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FedCausal-Dyn, a novel federated learning framework addressing dynamic feature drift using causal-domain feature separation and reliability-weighted prototype aggregation, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to solve the issue of dynamic feature drift in federated learning, which challenges existing methods that assume static drift, limiting their adaptability in evolving environments.
AI.method: The framework uses causal-domain feature separation via projection heads and adversarial training. It introduces reliability-weighted prototype aggregation alongside causal-feature guided collaborative regularization.
AI.result: FedCausal-Dyn demonstrates state-of-the-art accuracy and stable results on three federated domain generalization benchmarks, verified by extensive experiments and ablation studies.
AI.conclusion: FedCausal-Dyn provides a robust, principled solution for handling non-stationary environments in federated learning by leveraging causal-domain feature separation and collaborative regularization.

title: RASR: Range-Aware Scale Recovery for Metric UAV Navigation
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures. Technical report for the UAVM 2026 PairUAV Challenge
summary: Under Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) denial, a UAV controller still needs a distance and heading command it can execute, making accurate metric last-meter navigation essential. Dense pair-geometry foundation models transfer relative structure well, yet the distance scale of their raw metric outputs remains poorly calibrated. Under the relative error metric of PairUAV, correcting only the average scale can still leave costly, distance-dependent residuals near the goal. To address this scale mismatch, Range-Aware Scale Recovery (RASR) separates a transferable scale-recovery core from a protocol-specific calibration module in a per-pair system fixed at inference. The core compresses frozen Matching And Stereo 3D Reconstruction (MASt3R)-style geometry into a compact descriptor and uses global calibration to recover the dominant metric signal. Range-bucket residual correction and command-grid alignment stay inside the calibration module, so they match the command format and evaluation protocol of PairUAV. On the UAVs in Multimedia 2026 PairUAV online evaluation, RASR reaches a total score of 0.003189. Under the PairUAV protocol, frozen pair geometry thus yields stable per-pair distance and heading estimates, while every protocol-specific adjustment stays confined to a calibration module fixed before inference. Code and materials are available at https://github.com/lht-research/rasr-pairuav.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of accurate navigation for UAVs under GNSS denial by introducing Range-Aware Scale Recovery (RASR), which calibrates the scale of metric outputs and adapts them to specific protocols.
AI.motivation: To enable UAVs to perform accurate distance and heading-based navigation under GNSS denial, overcoming scale calibration challenges inherent in dense pair-geometry models.
AI.method: The RASR framework introduces a two-step solution: compress dense geometry (e.g., MASt3R-style) into descriptors and employ a calibration module for context-specific adjustments like range-bucket correction.
AI.result: Using real-world PairUAV evaluations, RASR achieved a total score of 0.003189, demonstrating stable distance and heading accuracy across multiple UAVs' protocols.
AI.conclusion: RASR effectively calibrates metric outputs, ensuring precise navigation while isolating protocol-specific adjustments to a calibration module before inference. The solution is widely applicable and optimized for UAV tasks.

title: Adversarially Guided Diffusion for LiDAR Range Image Synthesis
comment: Accepted at the 1st Workshop on Secure and Trustworthy AI (STAI 2026), co-located with the European Conference on Machine Learning and Principles and Practice of Knowledge Discovery in Databases (ECML PKDD 2026)
summary: LiDAR semantic segmentation is a key perception task in autonomous driving, where false predictions can affect downstream planning and safety-critical decision-making. Although adversarial attacks, and specifically adversarial examples, have been widely studied for image classification and 3D point cloud segmentation, unrestricted adversarial examples remain largely unexplored in the space of 2D range images, which are projections of 3D point clouds. The proposed method is, to the best of our knowledge, the first diffusion-based unrestricted adversarial attack against 2D range-image segmentation, using adversarial guidance from a segmentation loss. By applying guidance directly during sampling, the method produces unrestricted adversarial examples that remain close to the learned LiDAR data manifold while inducing structured segmentation errors. Experiments on the SemanticKITTI dataset using RangeNet++ and CENet segmentation networks demonstrate that the attack provides adjustable degradation across guidance strengths and transfers across segmentation architectures. Compared with norm-bounded FGSM and SegPGD baselines, the proposed attack offers a distinct effectiveness-realism trade-off, achieving controllable white-box and transfer degradation while maintaining competitive distributional and visual realism.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel diffusion-based unrestricted adversarial attack for LiDAR 2D range-image segmentation, targeting autonomous driving systems.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving systems rely on accurate LiDAR semantic segmentation, and adversarial attacks could expose vulnerabilities impacting safety. Current research has gaps in studying unrestricted adversarial examples for 2D range images.
AI.method: The authors propose a diffusion-based approach that uses adversarial guidance from a segmentation loss during sampling, producing segmentation errors while keeping adversarial examples close to the LiDAR data manifold.
AI.result: Experiments on the SemanticKITTI dataset using RangeNet++ and CENet reveal that the attack degrades segmentation performance across different models and maintains better realism compared to norm-bounded baselines like FGSM and SegPGD.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method demonstrates an adjustable trade-off between attack effectiveness and realism, providing a novel way to test and enhance the robustness of autonomous driving perception systems.

title: Adaptive Model Compression (AMC): Saliency-Driven Resource Allocation for Ultra-Low-Power Transformer Inference
summary: Deploying large-scale transformer models on resource-constrained edge devices remains a challenge due to the high energy and memory overhead inherent in static inference, which processes simple and complex tokens with uniform intensity. To address this, we propose Adaptive Model Compression (AMC), a saliency-driven framework that dynamically allocates hardware resources based on token importance. By implementing a multi-tier architecture, our system identifies critical high-saliency information for full-precision processing while aggressively reducing the rank and bit-width of less significant data. Experimental results demonstrate that AMC achieves a 59.2% reduction in system energy and a 2.24x increase in throughput on 45nm CMOS hardware. This approach effectively extends the battery life of mobile devices by utilizing high-definition compute only where necessary, maintaining robust performance with a marginal 3.6% accuracy trade-off.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Adaptive Model Compression (AMC) to address the challenge of running large-scale transformer models on edge devices by dynamically allocating resources based on token importance.
AI.motivation: To solve the issue of high energy and memory costs in running static inference for transformer models on edge devices.
AI.method: The proposed AMC framework uses a multi-tier architecture to allocate hardware resources dynamically by processing high-saliency tokens in full precision and reducing rank/bit-width for less critical tokens.
AI.result: AMC achieves a 59.2% energy reduction and 2.24x throughput increase on 45nm CMOS hardware while maintaining performance with just a 3.6% accuracy trade-off.
AI.conclusion: AMC demonstrates the feasibility of deploying resource-efficient transformer models on edge devices, enhancing battery life and throughput without significant accuracy compromises.

title: Xema: Efficient Diffusion Serving through Fine-Grained Memory Management and Auto-Configuration
summary: Diffusion models are increasingly deployed as production visual-generation services, where serving high-resolution image and long video generation is often limited by GPU memory. Popular memory-saving techniques such as weight offloading, sharding, and VAE slicing are often not practical because they tend to introduce significant performance overhead. In this paper, we present Xema, a memory-efficient diffusion serving system that exploits predictable tensor lifetimes for trace-guided memory optimization. For each request template, Xema derives an offline memory trace to identify short memory-pressure intervals and applies memory mitigation only within these intervals and only by the amount needed to fit the target GPU budget. Xema further constructs a static memory layout for tensors with predictable lifetimes, reducing fragmentation-induced reserved memory and making offline memory reasoning reliable at runtime. Built on this memory optimization layer, Xema introduces an offline planner that jointly selects parallelism, concurrency, and memory control under GPU memory and SLO constraints. The selected plan is stored in a plan table and directly used by the online serving runtime. We implement Xema on production diffusion pipelines and evaluate it with Flux.2, CogVideoX-5B, and LTX-2. Compared with existing serving configurations, Xema improves SLO attainment by up to 3.7x and reduces planning cost from 6.3 hours to 197 seconds compared with grid search.

AI.tldr: Xema is a novel memory-efficient system specially designed for serving high-resolution diffusion models. It addresses GPU memory constraints without the significant performance costs of conventional methods by optimizing tensor lifetimes and fine-tuning memory use.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to mitigate GPU memory limitations that challenge high-resolution image and long video generation in diffusion models, while maintaining system efficiency and performance standards.
AI.method: Xema utilizes trace-guided memory optimization, analyzing predictable tensor lifetimes for offline memory planning. It deploys dynamic memory mitigation during short pressure intervals and creates static memory layouts to reduce fragmentation. Additionally, it adopts an offline planner to optimize parallelism, concurrency, and memory management.
AI.result: Xema significantly improves SLO attainment (up to 3.7x higher) and reduces planning time from hours to mere seconds, demonstrating practicality and superiority over traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: Xema offers an efficient and effective solution for serving diffusion models within GPU memory constraints, enabling scalable visual-generation services with optimized performance.

title: Distributed Semantics for Distributed Quantum Computing
summary: We present a quantum process calculus that can split the system state along process boundaries and follow the evolution of each process in isolation, without losing information about the joint state-a property we call spatial compositionality. Compositionality is the key to reasoning about any complex system, yet quantum process calculi have struggled to provide its spatial kind, which would enable analyzing a system one process at a time. Many a quantum process calculi have been proposed, but they invariably rely on a global state representation based on state vectors or density matrices, with no known way to split them without losing information about entanglement. We propose to model quantum states with Deutsch-Hayden descriptors instead, which provide a modular representation of qubit states and their evolution. We adapt these descriptors to allow arbitrary splitting and merging of the store of qubits, leading to an unusual process calculus in which qubit transfer messages carry the actual state of the qubit, where existing calculi transfer only a reference. The calculus gives localized views of system state visible to each process, which can be assembled back together into the joint state. We define a notion of process equivalence with extensive justification grounded in physics and show a bisimulation whose soundness proof is simplified by spatial compositionality. The calculus can model open systems entangled with external processes, and we demonstrate this capability on a fragment of the BB84 key distribution protocol. This exercise shows that Deutsch-Hayden descriptors can successfully track qubit movements across process and system boundaries, though it needs help from density matrices to reason about information flow.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a quantum process calculus based on spatial compositionality, using Deutsch-Hayden descriptors to divide state information among processes while preserving entanglement details.
AI.motivation: Current quantum process calculi struggle to model systems in a spatially compositional manner, as they rely on global state representations that cannot manage entanglement efficiently when split.
AI.method: The authors propose a calculus that employs Deutsch-Hayden descriptors for modular qubit state representation, enabling splitting, merging, and localized state views while preserving entanglement. Process equivalences are defined and supported by a bisimulation framework and physical justification.
AI.result: The proposed calculus enables tracking of quantum states across processes, even open systems, and is demonstrated on a part of the BB84 protocol, where it successfully tracks qubit interactions.
AI.conclusion: Spatial compositionality is a key advancement for reasoning in quantum systems, and the use of Deutsch-Hayden descriptors highlights a promising pathway, although supplementary tools like density matrices remain necessary for some analyses.

title: Mitigating LLM Sycophancy in Code Smell Detection Using Evidence-Guided Reasoning Prompts
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used for code smell detection tasks due to their ability to interpret program semantics. However, their reliability in this context remains poorly explored, particularly under varying prompt conditions where model predictions may be influenced by external cues rather than code characteristics. One such limitation is sycophancy bias, where models tend to align their outputs with user-provided assumptions instead of performing objective analysis. In this paper, we present the first systematic empirical study of sycophancy bias in LLM-based code smell detection. Using the MLCQ dataset, we evaluate how different prompt framings like confirmation bias, contradictory hints, and false premises affect model predictions. Our results show that LLMs are highly sensitive to prompt variations, with Decision Flip Rates reaching up to 72% and False Alignment Rates exceeding 90%, indicating substantial instability and agreement with misleading prompts. To address this issue, we propose Evidence-Guided Debiasing Prompting (EGDP), a structured prompting strategy that enforces evidence-first reasoning. EGDP reduces decision instability and improves robustness, lowering Decision Flip Rates to as low as 12% and False Alignment Rates to as low as 21%, while increasing reliance on structurally grounded evidence. Our findings demonstrate that sycophancy bias poses a critical threat to the reliability of LLM-based code smell detection, and that evidence-guided reasoning provides an effective and generalizable mitigation approach.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates sycophancy bias in LLMs for code smell detection and proposes a strategy to mitigate it.
AI.motivation: To explore and address the reliability issue of LLMs in code smell detection due to sycophancy bias and their sensitivity to prompt variations.
AI.method: The authors conducted an empirical study using the MLCQ dataset and evaluated the impact of prompt framings (e.g., confirmation bias, false premises) on LLM predictions. They also introduced Evidence-Guided Debiasing Prompting (EGDP), a structured strategy emphasizing evidence-first reasoning.
AI.result: The study found that LLMs are highly susceptible to prompt-induced biases, with Decision Flip Rates up to 72% and False Alignment Rates exceeding 90%. EGDP reduced these rates to 12% and 21%, respectively, while improving evidence-based reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Sycophancy bias significantly compromises the reliability of LLMs in code smell detection, but evidence-guided reasoning (EGDP) offers an effective and generalizable solution to mitigate this issue.

title: A Dynamic Scene Interaction Reasoning Framework for Scene-level Lane-Change Intention and Trajectory Prediction of Multiple Interacting Vehicles
summary: Safe motion planning in advanced driver-assistance systems and autonomous vehicles requires an accurate understanding of how the surrounding traffic scene is likely to evolve. However, many existing lane-change prediction methods remain centered on a single target vehicle, while multi-agent forecasting approaches often describe scene evolution only through future positions and provide limited explicit information about the maneuver associated with each vehicle. This study proposes a dynamic scene graph attention framework that predicts the lane-change intention and future trajectory of every relevant vehicle within a local traffic scene. The scene is represented as a time-varying interaction graph in which vehicles are modeled as nodes and their spatial and kinematic relationships are encoded through explicit edge features. Temporal graph-attention message passing captures evolving inter-vehicle dependencies and pre-maneuver cues, while an intention-guided decoder links each predicted maneuver to its corresponding future motion. A scene-level consistency objective further encourages compatible multi-vehicle futures. Experiments on the NGSIM I-80, NGSIM US-101, and highD datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over competing baselines. DSiGAT achieves intention prediction accuracies of 90.12% and 90.97% on NGSIM I-80 and US-101, respectively, and reduces trajectory RMSE by up to 52.94% relative to the strongest baseline. It also produces lower inter-agent collision rates and joint displacement errors, indicating more coherent scene-level predictions. Ablation, sensitivity, robustness, and qualitative analyses further validate the contribution of the proposed components and the effectiveness of the scene-focused formulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework that predicts lane-change intentions and future trajectories for all vehicles in a traffic scene using a dynamic scene graph attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing lane-change prediction methods that either focus on single vehicles or lack explicit maneuver information for multiple vehicles, making it challenging to understand dynamic traffic scenes accurately.
AI.method: The study introduces a dynamic scene graph attention framework that represents traffic as a time-varying interaction graph. Vehicles are nodes, spatial/kinematic relationships are edge features, and temporal graph-attention captures vehicle dependencies. An intention-guided decoder links maneuvers to predicted motions, and a scene-level consistency objective ensures compatible multi-vehicle predictions.
AI.result: The proposed method, DSiGAT, achieved higher intention prediction accuracies (90.12% on NGSIM I-80 and 90.97% on US-101), reduced trajectory RMSE by up to 52.94%, and lowered inter-agent collision rates and joint displacement errors. The method consistently outperformed existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: DSiGAT demonstrates that incorporating a dynamic scene graph framework enhances scene-level traffic predictions, improving both maneuver accuracy and trajectory coherence, while maintaining robustness and compatibility across multiple vehicles.

title: Conservation Laws for Diffusion Models
summary: While autoregressive models optimize the exact data likelihood via the chain rule, diffusion models are typically trained with denoising objectives. We develop conservation laws based on generalized extrinsic information transfer (GEXIT) functions for a broad class of memoryless noise processes, showing that the data--model cross-entropy (CE) can be characterized exactly as an integral of local information-theoretic derivatives along the noise path. This yields a unified characterization of the likelihood for discrete and continuous diffusion, with the Gaussian case reducing to the well-known mutual information--minimum mean-square error (I-MMSE) relationship. An immediate implication is a locality property: one can compute the information-theoretic derivatives using only the marginal posteriors along the noise path. As a result, training reduces to learning the marginal posteriors by minimizing the negative log-likelihood. While the conservation law implies that the entropy does not depend on the noise path, finite-capacity denoisers approximate the posteriors with varying accuracy across noise types, leading to differences in performance. We validate these predictions on synthetic Markov sources and standard benchmarks, including text8 and CIFAR-10.

AI.tldr: This paper derives conservation laws to unify the training of diffusion models and autoregressive methods through an information-theoretic framework, connecting data likelihood and denoising objectives.
AI.motivation: To establish a unified method to characterize the behavior of diffusion models and autoregressive models by better understanding the connection between data likelihood and denoising objectives, addressing differences in performance across noise types.
AI.method: Utilized generalized extrinsic information transfer (GEXIT) functions to derive conservation laws applicable to noise processes, characterizing cross-entropy as an integral of local information-theoretic derivatives along a noise path. The theoretical framework was validated with synthetic Markov sources and real benchmarks for discrete and continuous diffusion.
AI.result: The study identified a relationship between entropy and noise path independence while identifying finite-capacity denoisers' approximation differences. The results were validated on datasets like text8 and CIFAR-10, confirming performance predictions.
AI.conclusion: The conservation law provides a theoretically grounded, unified framework for training diffusion models by linking likelihood to entropy, offering insights into the performance difference due to noise paths in finite-capacity models.

title: Consensus vs. Dissent: Dynamic LLM Modeling of Subjective Preferences in Group Recommenders
comment: Full paper accepted at the 20th ACM Conference on Recommender Systems (RecSys 2026)
summary:
Previous work in group recommender systems has demonstrated a sensitivity to the distribution of preferences within a group. Specifically, the selection of the preference aggregation strategy benefits from considering such group configurations. In this paper, we study whether LLMs are able to mimic this sensitivity and to select the ideal aggregation strategy (and corresponding recommendation) according to nuanced human perceptions of fairness, satisfaction, and consensus.
  We do this by fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) on human survey data to serve as real-time judgmental models within the recommendation pipeline. Using a reasoning dataset distilled from DeepSeek-V3.1 and human ground truth assessments, we develop Judgmental Llama and Judgmental OLMo to simulate group assessments. Our pipeline successfully generates multiple recommendation candidates based on social choice-based aggregation strategies and dynamically selects the one that maximizes these predicted human-like evaluations. We further validate these suggestions in a user study (n=284) and find that our methodology achieved the highest scores for satisfaction and group consensus. Furthermore, we find that LLM judgments are most aligned with human perceptions of fairness, satisfaction and consensus when we also consider interaction effects between our LLM-based method and group configuration (e.g., minority or coalition). These findings give further support for dynamically adapting aggregation strategies to specific within-group preference distributions, and highlight the advantage of using LLMs for an adaptation that is aligned with subjective human judgments.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the capability of fine-tuned Large Language Models (LLMs) to mimic human-like sensitivity in selecting optimal aggregation strategies for group recommendations, focusing on fairness, satisfaction, and consensus.
AI.motivation: The study aims to explore whether LLMs can replicate nuanced human judgments in group recommender systems, particularly in the selection of preference aggregation strategies that align with fairness, satisfaction, and group consensus.
AI.method: The approach involves fine-tuning LLMs (Judgmental Llama and Judgmental OLMo) on human survey data to act as real-time judgmental models. These models assess and identify the best aggregation strategies in the recommendation pipeline using reasoning datasets and a validation user study.
AI.result: The proposed methodology achieved the highest scores in satisfaction and group consensus when validated with real users. LLM judgments closely aligned with human perceptions, especially when accounting for group configurations like minority and coalition effects.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates the value of dynamically adapting aggregation strategies to specific group preference distributions using fine-tuned LLMs. These models provide human-like evaluations aligned with fairness, satisfaction, and consensus, representing a step forward in personalized group recommender systems.

title: Mitigating Early Training Collapse in CTR Models
summary: Deep neural models for click-through rate prediction often exhibit a sharp decline in validation performance immediately after the first training epoch despite continued improvement in training loss. This instability restricts effective learning and limits model performance. In this study, we analyze this behavior using large-scale industrial datasets and evaluate practical mitigation strategies. While reducing the learning rate provides only incremental gains, controlling feature sparsity yields substantial improvements. Removing highly sparse features and aggregating infrequent feature values stabilizes training, extends useful learning beyond a single epoch, and improves both offline evaluation metrics and online system performance.

AI.tldr: Deep neural models for click-through rate prediction suffer from a swift decline in validation performance post the first epoch despite better training loss. The paper proposes strategies to mitigate this issue.
AI.motivation: To address instability in training deep neural models for click-through rate prediction, which restricts efficient learning and performance.
AI.method: The study analyzed the behavior on large-scale industrial datasets and proposed controlling feature sparsity through removing highly sparse features and aggregating infrequent feature values.
AI.result: Reducing learning rate had minimal improvements, but controlling feature sparsity stabilized training, extended meaningful training beyond one epoch, and enhanced both offline and online metrics.
AI.conclusion: Effective management of feature sparsity alleviates training instability and boosts model performance in click-through rate prediction tasks.

title: TS-Mask VLA: 2D Temporal-Spatial Masking for Vision-Language-Action Model with Effective Bridging
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, accepted to the 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2026)
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models aim to understand natural-language instructions and visual observations, and to generate and execute corresponding actions as embodied agents. Recently, autoregressive token-based action generation has driven the development of many representative VLA models. However, this paradigm often reduces action generation to next-token prediction, thereby lacking explicit modeling of the spatiotemporal structure of action sequences and the disentanglement between vision-language representations and actions, which can limit performance in long-horizon and complex scenarios. In this paper, we propose TS-Mask VLA, a vision-language-action framework for robot manipulation. TS-Mask VLA is built upon two key designs: (1) a Discrete Diffusion Action Expert equipped with a Bridge Attention conditioning bridge, which enables multi-layer conditioning from the VLM and facilitates more accurate and stable action generation; and (2) a temporal-spatial 2D masking strategy for discrete action tokens that strengthens the model's understanding of cross-time dependencies and inter-dimensional coupling, leading to more structurally consistent action sequences. We conduct extensive experiments on simulation benchmarks and real-world tasks. On LIBERO, TS-Mask VLA achieves a 95.7 percent average success rate with only 0.5B parameters, outperforming significantly larger models. On CALVIN, it attains the best average sequence length of 4.19 and strong long-horizon performance. Comprehensive analyses and ablations further validate the effectiveness of our design.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TS-Mask VLA, a framework for vision-language-action models, enhancing action generation for robotic manipulation using discrete diffusion and temporal-spatial masking.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models struggle with spatiotemporal structure modeling and disentangling vision-language from actions, hindering performance in complex, long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: Propose two innovations: (1) A Discrete Diffusion Action Expert with multi-layered Bridge Attention conditioning, and (2) a temporal-spatial 2D masking strategy to enhance action sequence understanding and structural consistency.
AI.result: TS-Mask VLA outperforms larger models on LIBERO with a 95.7% success rate and excels in long-horizon performance on CALVIN with the best sequence length of 4.19.
AI.conclusion: TS-Mask VLA provides a significant step forward in VLA model design by improving action sequence consistency and performance through novel methodologies, validated by extensive experiments and ablation studies.

title: MVMGNN;Multi-View Masked Graph Neural Network for Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis using Structural MRI
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a common neurodegenerative disorder, and early diagnosis is of great significance for delaying disease progression and enabling timely intervention. Mild cognitive impairment (MCI), which represents an intermediate clinical stage between cognitively normal aging and AD. Structural magnetic resonance imaging (sMRI) provides detailed characterization of anatomical structures and plays an important role in AD-related brain analysis. However, existing sMRI-based brain network methods typically rely on a single graph construction strategy, limiting their ability to jointly capture spatial relationships and morphological similarities between brain regions. To address these issues, this paper proposes an sMRI-based multi-view masked graph neural network model (MVMGNN) for AD diagnosis. A joint node-edge masking mechanism is proposed to simultaneously select radiomics feature dimensions and structural connections, reducing redundancy during graph learning. Furthermore, a patient-level cross-view gated fusion mechanism is proposed to integrate multi-view representations. Experimental results on the ADNI dataset demonstrate that MVMGNN outperforms several competing approaches in AD classification. Interpretability analysis further demonstrates that MVMGNN is able to identify key brain regions associated with AD, providing useful insights into discriminative patterns in sMRI-based brain networks.Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/chenzhao2023/MVMGNN_AD

AI.tldr: The paper presents an advanced model (MVMGNN) for Alzheimer's diagnosis using multi-view sMRI-based brain networks, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Early diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease (AD) is critical to delay progression and enable timely intervention, but current sMRI-based methods inadequately capture both spatial relationships and morphological similarities of brain networks.
AI.method: The proposed method, MVMGNN, uses a joint node-edge masking mechanism to reduce redundancy in graph learning and a patient-level cross-view gated fusion mechanism to merge multi-view representations.
AI.result: Experimental results on the ADNI dataset showcase MVMGNN's superior performance in AD classification compared to competing approaches.
AI.conclusion: MVMGNN demonstrates improved accuracy in AD diagnosis and provides interpretability by identifying key brain regions associated with the disease, offering promising insights for sMRI-based brain network analysis.

title: GPU-Tile-Sim: A Tile-Centric GPU Simulation Framework for LLM Hardware-Software Co-Design
summary: Modern LLM (large language model) workloads increasingly rely on optimized GPU kernels through hardware-software co-design. These kernels achieve high-performance through fine-grained dependency scheduling and computation-memory overlap. As such, they incur new challenges on existing GPU performance models. Instruction-driven simulators are costly to adapt to evolving architectures, while analytical models are too coarse to capture kernels' characteristics. We propose GPU-Tile-Sim, a tile-centric GPU simulation framework for LLM hardware-software co-design. The key insight is that modern LLM kernel performance is governed less by individual instruction latency than by the dependency structure that controls execution order and overlap. Accordingly, GTSim represents kernel execution as a warp-level tile graph whose nodes capture tile-level operations and whose edges encode data and ordering constraints. Using this representation, we design an automatic tile-graph frontend and a graph-driven simulation backend. We evaluate GTSim on representative GEMM, attention, and end-to-end LLM inference workloads. On A100 and H100 across both conventional and highly optimized kernels, GTSim achieves high performance-modeling accuracy (MAPE, Mean Absolute Percentage Error, 1.22%--8.71%). We further extend GTSim to Blackwell with preliminary validation, and demonstrate its effectiveness in analyzing software and architectural design choices.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GPU-Tile-Sim (GTSim), a tile-centric GPU simulation framework tailored for modern LLM workloads, achieving high accuracy in performance modeling.
AI.motivation: Existing GPU performance models struggle to effectively handle modern LLM workloads due to their fine-grained dependency scheduling and computation-memory overlap. This paper aims to address these challenges with a novel framework.
AI.method: The authors propose GTSim, which uses a warp-level tile graph representation to simulate GPU kernel execution. This framework includes an automatic tile-graph frontend and a graph-driven simulation backend.
AI.result: The evaluation demonstrates that GTSim achieves a high degree of accuracy (MAPE 1.22%–8.71%) for various workloads on A100 and H100 GPUs and provides insights for software and architectural design for LLM workloads.
AI.conclusion: GTSim effectively addresses the limitations of existing GPU performance models and offers a robust tool for LLM hardware-software co-design analysis.

title: Compile, Then Page: Executable SOP Programs and a Capability-Gated Runtime for Procedural LLM Agents
summary: Enterprise agents must follow long-horizon, conditional, safety-critical standard operating procedures (SOPs). We compile machine-readable SOP constraints into executable pseudo-code and run them with a program-guided (PG) stack machine that pages the active frame while an LLM performs semantic execution. A three-arm SOPBench study across six models separates representation from runtime: compiled text never significantly hurts and gains up to 16.0 points where official prose underperforms. Runtime guidance is capability-gated. Two strong models independently show positive seven-domain PG contrasts (58:19 and 75:31 discordant pairs), whereas weak models are harmed. A full-program cursor ablation (active frame first, complete program retained) recovers much of the strong-model refusal gain; selective visibility adds a smaller improvement. Paired probe and audit measurements track this divide to spontaneous state discipline rather than reconstruction ability. On Bank the three primary arms rise from 70.4 to 86.4 to 92.8, with 100% refusal correctness. Practical guidance: compile first; enable active-frame paging only after a model-level discipline check.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method to handle long-horizon, safety-critical standard operating procedures (SOPs) using program-guided (PG) stack machines and large language models (LLMs), achieving notable improvements under controlled conditions.
AI.motivation: Developing a method to ensure enterprise agents can effectively follow complex, conditional, and safety-critical SOPs while overcoming limitations in typical LLM-based approaches.
AI.method: The paper compiles SOP constraints into executable pseudo-code and uses a program-guided (PG) stack machine to interact with a large language model (LLM) for semantic execution. The approach is tested across six models using a benchmark study (SOPBench) with various runtime and ablation strategies.
AI.result: Using program guidance, strong models show positive performance across seven domains, achieving higher accuracy and correct refusal rates. Compiling textual SOPs into pseudo-code improved execution significantly in cases where original prose failed.
AI.conclusion: The study recommends compiling SOPs into a machine-readable format and ensuring active-frame paging is enabled only with models that demonstrate state discipline, providing significant practical benefits for implementing SOPs in enterprise systems.

title: Is Model Instability just Noise to be Tolerated or a Property that can be Managed?
summary:
In software analytics, rerunning the same analysis twice often yields different models and conclusions. This reduces trust in the model and limits its use. We find that model instability is a major problem. Across 127 multi-objective SE optimization problems (12,700 test cases), repeated runs of a state-of-the-art optimizer agree on only 13.7% of test cases, even under improved settings. We argue that this instability is not merely noise to tolerate, but a property that can be measured and managed. By adjusting how labels are spent, how complex the models become, and how splits are scored, we obtain models that agree 4.8 times as often as the default configuration. The standard deviation of optimization error falls by 22% on average (mean std 17.4 to 13.6), while recommendation quality improves rather than degrades. In terms of quality, the refined settings are statistically top-ranked on 119 of 127 datasets, compared to 74 for the defaults. We then test causal and data-locality interventions and find that they help only partially, suggesting a residual stability floor. Our evidence suggests there are fundamental limits to stability set by the data itself (noise, scarce labels, proxy objectives, and the many near-equivalent models a dataset admits). We conclude that instability should be treated as a standard evaluation axis in SE optimization, which should be routinely measured, reported alongside performance, and used to calibrate trust in any single run. The methods in this paper provide a baseline against which future efforts to reduce SBSE instability can be judged.
  To support open science, we offer the following reproduction package: https://tinyurl.com/Model-Instability

AI.tldr: In software analytics, model instability is a major issue. Authors improved the agreement rate between models and reduced error variability using refined settings, while highlighting remaining challenges tied to data properties.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by the observation that repeated runs of software analytics often produce inconsistent models and conclusions, reducing trust and utility in such systems.
AI.method: The authors analyzed 127 SE optimization problems, testing interventions like label adjustments, model complexity control, and split scoring to improve model stability. They also explored causal and data-locality interventions.
AI.result: The proposed refinements increased model agreement by 4.8 times and reduced standard deviation in optimization errors by 22%. Recommendation quality improved and performance was statistically superior on most datasets (119 of 127).
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that model instability in SE optimization needs to be systematically measured, reported, and managed. This is essential for calibrated trust, and the provided methods offer a benchmark for reducing instability in future work.

title: Scaffolding the Strategist: Architecture-Dependent Reasoning Interventions in Hotelling Spatial Markets
comment: 26 pages (11 main + 15 appendix), 6 figures, 4 tables. Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on LLM Reasoning
summary: We investigate whether structured reasoning interventions improve the strategic economic reasoning of large language models, and whether their effects depend on model architecture. Using Hotelling's linear city model as a diagnostic vehicle, we evaluate GPT-4.1-mini (a standard instruction-following model) and GPT-5-mini (a reasoning-optimized model) under five conditions - an unscaffolded baseline and four reasoning interventions - across eight questions spanning deductive and abductive reasoning, three prompt framings, and three repetitions per condition, yielding 720 individually judged responses. We find a statistically significant crossover interaction between scaffolding type and model architecture ($t(7) = 4.79$, $p = 0.002$, $d = 1.69$): commitment scaffolding improves the standard model ($+0.21$) while degrading the reasoning model ($-0.63$), and principled separation shows the opposite pattern ($-0.40$ vs. $+0.31$). Both crossovers are individually significant (commitment: $p = 0.040$; separation: $p = 0.002$) and hold across all eight questions with 7/8 directional consistency. Adversarial stress-testing harms both models, with $2.6\times$ greater degradation for the reasoning model ($-1.47$ vs. $-0.57$; $p = 0.038$), and the damage correlates negatively with baseline difficulty ($R^2 = 0.36$, $p = 0.014$). We further document a persistent declarative-procedural gap in which both models identify correct strategies at rates far exceeding their ability to execute them; separation fully closes this gap for the reasoning model while no intervention helps the standard model.

AI.tldr: This study examines whether reasoning interventions improve strategic economic reasoning in GPT-4.1-mini and GPT-5-mini models using Hotelling's linear city model. Results show a significant interaction between intervention type and model architecture, revealing differing effectiveness based on model design.
AI.motivation: To understand whether structured reasoning interventions can enhance the ability of language models to perform strategic economic reasoning, and examine if the model architecture influences effectiveness.
AI.method: The study compares GPT-4.1-mini and GPT-5-mini across five conditions (baseline and four reasoning interventions), using questions requiring deductive and abductive reasoning, prompt framings, and multiple repetitions, totaling 720 judged responses.
AI.result: Commitment scaffolding improves GPT-4.1-mini but worsens GPT-5-mini, while principled separation shows the opposite. Adversarial stress-testing negatively impacts both models but affects GPT-5-mini more. Key reasoning gaps persist, but targeted interventions close the gap for GPT-5-mini.
AI.conclusion: Structured reasoning interventions impact large language models differently depending on architecture, with implications for optimizing reasoning tasks. Specific interventions may improve strategic performance in more reasoning-focused models but degrade it in standard ones.

title: Energy-guided Recursive Model
summary: Recursive reasoning models address structured problems by repeatedly updating latent states of small neural networks. However, their test-time scaling lacks a principled inference mechanism: increasing depth or stochastic breadth generates more trajectories without a clear criterion for selection, and existing methods predominantly rely on additional q-heads or heuristic voting. Here, we develop the Energy-guided Recursive Model (ERM), which introduces an intrinsic selection principle based on explicit Hopfield energies. ERM leverages Hopfield-type memories of valid local or global structures to define the selector over candidate trajectories. The resulting energy seamlessly integrates with energy-based techniques such as parallel tempering to enhance sampling efficiency and ranking. With $D=64$ recurrent steps and $K=128$ candidates, ERM reaches optimal solutions on Sudoku ($98.97\%$), Pencil Puzzle Bench (PPBench, $88.04\%$) and Maze ($99.30\%$), improving upon recent Probabilistic Tiny Recursive Model and Equilibrium Reasoners. These results suggest that incorporating explicit energy functions into recursive reasoning offers a principled path toward more effective inference.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Energy-guided Recursive Model (ERM), which improves test-time scaling in recursive reasoning models by implementing Hopfield-type energy-based selection criteria.
AI.motivation: Recursive reasoning models struggle with selecting optimal trajectories during test-time scaling due to the lack of a principled inference mechanism.
AI.method: ERM uses explicit Hopfield energies as an intrinsic criterion for selecting valid local or global structures among candidate trajectories. It integrates this energy with techniques like parallel tempering to enhance sampling and ranking.
AI.result: ERM achieves state-of-the-art results on benchmarks such as Sudoku (98.97% accuracy), Pencil Puzzle Bench (88.04% accuracy), and Maze (99.30% accuracy).
AI.conclusion: The integration of explicit energy functions into recursive reasoning models provides a more principled and effective approach for inference, outperforming previous models like Probabilistic Tiny Recursive Model and Equilibrium Reasoners.

title: PTEI: Integrating Personality Traits to Enhance Emotional Intelligence in Large Language Models
summary: Despite advances in Emotional Intelligence (EI), Large Language Models (LLMs) still significantly underperform humans in complex emotional reasoning. This gap originates partly from the limited incorporation of individual differences, particularly personality traits, which are fundamental to human emotional inference. To address this, we propose PTEI, a novel framework for integrating Personality Traits into Emotional Intelligence tasks using LLMs. In PTEI, MBTI and OCEAN personality traits are first extracted directly from the given emotional scenarios and then utilized as contextual knowledge within personality-aware prompts, guiding LLMs to accurately infer emotions and their underlying causes. To ensure optimal contextual grounding, we employ Contrastive Learning to construct an optimized retrieval system that surfaces emotionally and personally aligned scenarios, enhancing reasoning quality. Extensive experiments on established EI benchmarks show that PTEI enhances the Emotional Understanding (EU) capabilities of various LLMs, with the strongest improvement observed in GPT models. Combining PTEI with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning yields an additional 4 percent increase in accuracy. These findings underscore PTEI's contribution toward advancing AI systems with more sophisticated social and psychological grounding.

AI.tldr: PTEI enhances Emotional Intelligence in LLMs by incorporating personality traits, thereby improving their accuracy in emotional reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with complex emotional reasoning due to the lack of integration of individual differences like personality traits, which are crucial for emotional inference.
AI.method: PTEI extracts MBTI and OCEAN personality traits from emotional contexts and uses personality-aware prompts. It also employs Contrastive Learning to retrieve emotionally and personally relevant scenarios.
AI.result: Experiments show enhanced Emotional Understanding with LLMs, particularly GPT models, with an additional accuracy increase using Chain-of-Thought reasoning.
AI.conclusion: PTEI significantly improves the social and psychological grounding of AI systems, bridging a gap in emotional intelligence capabilities.

title: Safe responses matter: Output-aware safety guardrail mitigate over-refusal in MLLMs
summary: Existing safety mechanisms for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) face a fundamental trade-off between safety and utility. Model fine-tuning achieves robust safety but compromises general utility. Input-side safety guardrails offer a lightweight alternative, yet they suffer from severe over-refusal, indiscriminately blocking benign queries or those the model could have safely answered through refusal or advisory responses. We identify that the root cause of over-refusal lies in the input-aware paradigm: safety guardrails make safety decisions without considering whether the model itself is capable of generating safe responses. Usually, MLLMs already possess intrinsic safety mechanisms that can transform harmful inputs into harmless outputs, but input-side safety guardrails override this capability, degrading user experience. Motivated by this insight, we propose a paradigm shift toward output-aware safety guardrails. Our method operates within the model's hidden state space to predict whether the forthcoming generation will be unsafe before it is fully produced. By training a lightweight classifier via multi-instance contrastive learning on hidden state representations, our approach distinguishes between inputs that will lead to unsafe outputs and those that will not, even when the inputs themselves contain risky elements. This enables precise intervention only when the model's actual response would be harmful. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our output-aware safety guardrail matches the safety performance of existing methods while drastically reducing over-refusal, preserving the model's utility and built-in safety capabilities. Code is available at: https://github.com/kunzhan/OutGuard

AI.tldr: Existing safety measures for MLLMs compromise between robust safety and utility. The proposed output-aware safety guardrails improve precision by predicting unsafe outputs before they're generated.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of balancing safety and utility in multimodal large language models. Over-refusal caused by input-side safety mechanisms deteriorates user experience and prevents leveraging the model's intrinsic safety mechanisms.
AI.method: The researchers developed output-aware safety guardrails based on hidden state predictions. Using multi-instance contrastive learning, they trained a classifier to preemptively detect unsafe responses by analyzing the model's hidden state space.
AI.result: Experiments showed the proposed method matches safety performance of existing methods while reducing over-refusal and preserving the model's utility and intrinsic safety mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Output-aware safety guardrails resolve the trade-off between safety and utility in MLLMs by enabling targeted intervention based on response predictions, improving safety without compromising user experience.

title: More Structure, Not More Capacity: Object-Centric Representations for Visuomotor Imitation Learning
summary: Robotic manipulation policies rely on pre-trained vision models that give either a global scene embedding or a dense patch grid. Both mix task-relevant and task-irrelevant features. Object-centric slot representations are a structured alternative: they group features into a few per-object slots. We test what this structure buys on ManiSkill3 PickCube-v1, with a frozen encoder and a held-out-seed evaluation.
