In a $124$-million-parameter GPT-2 transformer whose weights are constrained to the $8$-bit floating-point grid after every optimizer step, with no master weights, the dense weights freeze at initialization in both fp8 formats -- predicted \emph{a priori} from an fp32 reference -- and validation loss plateaus while full precision keeps improving. Stochastic rounding removes the persistent freeze, and the same reference predicts that too. The condition transfers across frozen-feature regression, a mantissa-truncation emulator spanning $128\times$ in precision, small networks, and a CNN on MNIST: a computable axis of low-precision training, not diffuse noise.

AI.tldr: Training in reduced floating-point precision can cause a halting of learning when weight updates become smaller than the smallest resolvable unit for that precision, leading to ‘freezing’ of parameters.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates the risks and characteristics of training deep learning models in reduced precision, focusing on cases where precision limitations lead to model parameters freezing prematurely.
AI.method: The authors examine the deterministic nature of freezing by predicting where it will occur with a high-precision trajectory and evaluating it across different models, including GPT and CNNs, and with varying precisions (e.g., bf16, fp8). Stochastic rounding is tested as a potential solution.
AI.result: Parameter freezing in reduced precision was observed in multiple experiments, such as small GPTs and a GPT-2 transformer, with results closely matching predictions. Stochastic rounding successfully mitigates freezing.
AI.conclusion: Reduced-precision training introduces a predictable and deterministic freezing phenomenon that can undermine learning. Addressing this issue (e.g., via stochastic rounding) is crucial for effective use of low-precision training methods.

title: Runtime Safety Filtering for Learned Small UAS Separation Policies under GNSS Degradation
comment: Accepted for publication at the 2026 IEEE/AIAA Digital Avionics Systems Conference (DASC). 9 pages, 8 figures
summary: Learning-based separation assurance for small Unmanned Aircraft Systems (sUAS) achieves near-zero collision rates in simulation, but assumes accurate position and velocity information from Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS). This assumption fails in urban environments, where multipath propagation, signal blockage, and intentional interference degrade navigation integrity. This raises a fundamental architectural question for deploying learned separation policies under GNSS degradation: should runtime safety mechanisms filter the policy's actions or its observations? This work evaluates both approaches for multi-agent sUAS separation under adversarial GNSS degradation. Both architectures first estimate a worst-case traffic state consistent with bounded observation uncertainty, then diverge: action filtering constrains policy outputs via discrete-time control barrier functions evaluated at the worst-case state, while observation filtering presents the worst-case state directly to the policy as corrected input. Experimental results show that action filtering provides negligible safety improvement, while observation filtering reduces near mid-air collisions by 90% and remains robust to the barrier function's tradeoff between separation distance and closing rate. These results suggest that, for policies with learned safety behaviors, preserving the policy's decision authority outperforms overriding its actions with hand-designed constraints.

AI.tldr: The study compares action filtering and observation filtering for separation assurance in small drone systems under degraded GNSS conditions, finding that observation filtering significantly improves safety outcomes.
AI.motivation: To address safety concerns in small Unmanned Aircraft Systems (sUAS) when GNSS signals degrade due to urban challenges such as signal blockage and interference.
AI.method: Two architectures were evaluated: action filtering, which constrains policy outputs using control barrier functions, and observation filtering, which provides corrected worst-case scenarios directly as input to the policy.
AI.result: Observation filtering was 90% more effective at reducing near mid-air collisions and was robust to tradeoffs in maintaining separation distance and closing rate.
AI.conclusion: Preserving the decision-making autonomy of learned safety policies through observation filtering is more effective than overriding their actions with predefined constraints.

title: ShapKO: Shapley-Adaptive Modality Knockout for Robust Multimodal Learning
summary: Multimodal medical models often degrade when inputs are missing, a common scenario in real-world clinical workflows. Separately, even when all modalities are present, modality dominance is observed during training, where optimization over-relies on a highly predictive modality and undertrains complementary sources, resulting in poor robustness under partial availability. While training-time modality knockout improves missing-modality robustness, existing approaches use static masking rates that cannot adapt to evolving modality utility during training. We introduce ShapKO (Shapley-Adaptive Modality Knockout), a dynamic training strategy that learns modality-specific knockout probabilities based on validation utility. ShapKO periodically evaluates performance across modality subsets, estimates modality importance via Shapley values, and updates masking probabilities to suppress dominant modalities more frequently. This adaptive process promotes complementary representations, while requiring no architectural modifications. We evaluate ShapKO on three datasets covering multitask clinical classification, survival prediction, and cancer detection. ShapKO consistently improves performance under modality absence and yields interpretable trajectories of learned masking behavior. Code is available at: https://github.com/sumona00/ShapKO

AI.tldr: ShapKO is a training method to tackle issues of missing inputs and modality dominance in multimodal medical models by dynamically adjusting modality knockout probabilities based on Shapley values.
AI.motivation: Multimodal medical models face performance degradation when inputs are missing and suffer from modality dominance during training, leading to reduced robustness in real-world clinical workflows.
AI.method: The paper introduces ShapKO, a method that dynamically evaluates the utility of modalities using Shapley values and adjusts modality-specific knockout probabilities during training to suppress dominance and improve representations.
AI.result: ShapKO demonstrates consistent improvement in performance under conditions with missing modalities across three clinical datasets, covering diverse tasks such as classification, survival prediction, and cancer detection.
AI.conclusion: ShapKO enhances multimodal model robustness against missing inputs, avoids architectural changes, and promotes interpretability, offering a practical solution for robust clinical machine learning workflows.

title: BackendForge: Benchmarking Agentic End-to-End Code Generation with Backend Services
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in agentic coding settings, where they can inspect files, execute commands, run tests, observe failures, and iteratively revise code. This shift raises a central evaluation question: can an agentic LLM generate an end-to-end software artifact that is both deployable and behaviorally correct under execution? Backend services provide a controlled but realistic substrate for this evaluation. Their APIs expose application-level executable semantics, and deployed behavior can be checked deterministically against an OpenAPI contract through black-box HTTP interactions. We introduce BackendForge, a benchmark of 56 contract-defined backend generation tasks rewritten from real open-source applications. Given a visible specification and an OpenAPI contract, an LLM must generate a Dockerized service that is built, deployed, and evaluated only through HTTP tests. To strengthen evaluation without introducing hidden requirements, BackendForge uses a test agent and a code agent to co-evolve the test oracle and reference service, where the test agent proposes specification-grounded backend tests and the code agent repairs the reference implementation. Although the best-performing model, GPT-5.5, succeeds on 55.4\% of tasks under the base oracle, it succeeds on only 28.6\% under the final oracle. This gap suggests that current LLMs can implement many local API behaviors, but still struggle to produce complete backend services.

AI.tldr: Large language models (LLMs) are evaluated on their ability to generate deployable and behaviorally correct backend services via the BackendForge benchmark, highlighting persistent challenges in producing complete implementations.
AI.motivation: Assess whether agentic large language models can create end-to-end software artifacts that meet deployable and executable standards, especially in realistic coding scenarios.
AI.method: BackendForge benchmark tasks were designed using OpenAPI contracts and real-world specifications. Two agents—a test agent and a code agent—iteratively fine-tuned evaluation by co-evolving tests and reference implementations.
AI.result: GPT-5.5 achieved 55.4% success under a base oracle but only 28.6% under a stricter final oracle, showing limitations in creating comprehensive backend services.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs can handle local API behaviors well but encounter difficulties in generating fully deployable backend services, pointing to areas for improvement in LLM capabilities.

title: Verification of Adaptive Agentic Controllers through Finite Rule Revision
summary: Industrial agentic AI systems increasingly exhibit a gap between prototype capability and production deployment. In particular, adaptive agents may generate plausible outputs while remaining difficult to verify under non-determinism, confidentiality constraints, limited context, and weak observability. This paper formulates a bounded verification protocol for adaptive agentic controllers represented by finite symbolic rules, explicit diagnostic predicates, explanation logs, and held-out re-evaluation. The central research question is: when an adaptive agentic controller is represented through finite rules, explicit diagnostic predicates, explanation logs, and held-out re-evaluation, which classes of controller failure can be detected, locally repaired, or rejected without relying on unrestricted human-in-the-loop judgment? The proposed framework treats the controller as a finite revisable object. Diagnostic failures are mapped to predefined rule-level edits, including rule addition, rule deletion, and priority revision. Repaired controllers are then evaluated on held-out simulation seeds or cloned initial states. Experiments in a stylized financially constrained inventory-control benchmark show three outcomes: resource-induced failures that remain non-repairable by one rule edit, partial repairs that are rejected because they violate thresholds or guardrails, and a local one-step repair of an order-volatility failure induced by removing a smoothing rule. The contribution is methodological and provides a simulation-compatible procedure for testing whether specific controller-level failures can be made observable, explainable, locally revisable, and empirically re-tested under controlled conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses bounded verification of adaptive AI controllers using finite symbolic rules, diagnostic predicates, and re-evaluation on held-out data.
AI.motivation: The gap between prototype capability and real-world deployment of adaptive AI controllers due to issues like non-determinism and limited observability motivates the need for verification protocols.
AI.method: A framework is proposed where failures are diagnosed through symbolic rule revisions (e.g., addition, deletion, priority change) and tested against held-out simulations for their observability, explainability, and revisability.
AI.result: Tests on inventory-control benchmarks reveal different classes of failures: non-repairable failures, repairs violating thresholds, and successfully localized one-step repairs for specific issues.
AI.conclusion: The paper contributes a method for testing and revising adaptive controllers, ensuring their failures are observable, explainable, and locally repairable under experimental conditions.

title: Sticky Jump Diffusions: A Unifying View of Masked, Continuous, and Hybrid Diffusion
summary: We introduce Sticky Jump Diffusions (SJDs), continuous-time Markov processes on $\mathbb R^d$ whose discrete anchors are token embeddings. In forward time, anchors release their mass at a hazard rate and the released mass diffuses in the continuous ambient space; time reversal couples a score-driven SDE with a sticky jump kernel whose rate and destination are fixed by flux balance with the forward law. We estimate the score and the per-anchor reverse hazards from a single denoising classifier via Denoising Hazard Matching, the hazard analogue of denoising score matching, with simulation-free cross-entropy training. SJD recovers masked diffusion, continuous diffusion, and hybrid diffusion as limits. Its reversal explains features that each family treats as given: the mask of masked diffusion carries no evidence about the source token because the unsticking kernel of every anchor collapses to the same absorbing point; the terminal projection of continuous diffusion is required due to the absence of atoms in its forward marginal, without which flux balance yields no reverse jumps; and the update rules of hybrid diffusion (commit rate, destination, and drift) all follow from flux balance rather than from separate design. Beyond these limits, the unsticking kernel becomes a design space: a cross-position blending corrupts each position toward a blend of its neighbors' clean values or embeddings, turning dependency structure such as spatial locality or a constraint graph into an inductive bias of the corruption itself, and improves over the identity-kernel hybrid on CIFAR-10, Text8, and Sudoku.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Sticky Jump Diffusions (SJDs), a Markov process combining discrete token embeddings with continuous diffusion in ambient space and proposes methods for training and reverse-engineering its components evaluated on datasets like CIFAR-10 and Sudoku.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to unify and expand upon existing diffusion models such as masked diffusion and hybrid diffusion, explaining their features as outcomes of flux balance and offering a generalization with novel design possibilities.
AI.method: Sticky Jump Diffusions use a combination of a forward process that diffuses mass and a reverse process defined by score-driven SDEs and sticky jump kernels. The parameters are learned via a new method called Denoising Hazard Matching, trained through simulation-free cross-entropy.
AI.result: The proposed SJD model extends diffusion models with a tunable unsticking kernel and demonstrates improved performance over traditional models by incorporating inductive biases like spatial locality or constraints on datasets such as CIFAR-10, Text8, and Sudoku.
AI.conclusion: Sticky Jump Diffusions not only encompass existing diffusion types but also offer a flexible framework for incorporating domain knowledge into the diffusion process, achieving better modeling performance on structured and spatially-local datasets.

title: A Stepwise Questioning Expert-Editor Multi-Agent Framework for Long-Document Summarization
summary: Although large language models (LLMs) have shown promising potential in news summarization tasks, their performance on long-document summarization remains challenging as their length often exceeds the input limits. As the agent investment, which provide possibility to improve the inherent capabilities of LLMs. To enhance the effectiveness of long-document summarization based on LLMs, this paper proposes an expert-editor stepwise questioning multi-agent method, in which the expert and the editor guide another agent to refine the summary by posing questions on different aspects of the content and providing targeted clues for revision. We conducted experiments on two representative long-document scientific datasets and evaluated the results through widely recognized automatic metrics. The results demonstrated the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses long-document summarization issues for large language models by proposing a multi-agent method where an expert and editor guide another agent through stepwise questioning and revision.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the challenge of summarizing long documents with LLMs, given their input length limits, by improving their inherent summarization capabilities.
AI.method: The proposed expert-editor stepwise questioning multi-agent method involves leveraging an expert and an editor to collaboratively guide another agent in refining summaries, focusing on different document aspects and giving targeted revision clues.
AI.result: Experiments on two scientific datasets, using standard automatic metrics, prove the effectiveness of the proposed method.
AI.conclusion: The expert-editor multi-agent stepwise approach enhances the ability of LLMs to perform summaries of long documents effectively.

title: Discovering Latent Response Laws in Forced Physical Systems
comment: 23 pages, 5 figures and 2 tables. Supplementary Information provided as an ancillary file
summary: Governing equations provide compact descriptions of physical systems, yet the variables in which they are simple are often hidden in high-dimensional measurements. This challenge is sharper for forced systems, whose responses depend on both intrinsic dynamics and time-dependent inputs. Here we introduce FLARE, a forced latent autoencoder for response equations that learns compact response coordinates, identifies sparse input-dependent latent dynamics and decodes equation rollouts to full responses. By estimating latent dimension from data and separating state estimation from external forcing, FLARE enables forecasts to be initialized from past responses and driven by prescribed future inputs. Across known dynamical systems, application-scale forced responses and visual observations, FLARE recovers compact forced dynamics and predicts long-horizon high-dimensional responses under inputs not used for training. By turning learned coordinates into a dynamical interface, FLARE extends equation discovery to systems whose effective states are hidden within complex observations, providing a route for interpretable modelling and prediction of high-dimensional responses in forced dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FLARE, a method to extract and predict the hidden dynamics and responses of high-dimensional forced systems using learned compact coordinates.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of discovering underlying governing equations and compact response coordinates in complex forced dynamical systems based on high-dimensional measurements.
AI.method: FLARE, a latent autoencoder framework, discovers compact response coordinates, identifies input-dependent sparse latent dynamics, and decodes predictions into high-dimensional responses. It separates intrinsic dynamics from external forcing and estimates required latent dimensions directly from the data.
AI.result: FLARE was successful in recovering compact forced dynamics, predicting long-horizon responses for various known and complex systems under unseen inputs, and providing interpretable outputs.
AI.conclusion: FLARE significantly enhances the discovery and prediction of hidden dynamics in forced dynamical systems, offering a new route for interpretable modeling and forecasting high-dimensional responses.

title: Plug-and-Play Reweighting for Resilient Collaborative Decision-Making in Connected Autonomous Driving
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Accepted to the 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2026)
summary: Collaborative decision-making is a fundamental capability in multi-robot systems, such as connected autonomous vehicles. However, perceptual noise and adversarial attacks in collaborators can severely affect decision reliability. Overall, existing methods typically rely on retraining with attack-specific defenses or on restrictive perturbation assumptions to improve resilience, which limits their practicality. In this paper, we propose a novel Resilient Collaborative Decision-Making (RCDM) framework that consists of an attention-based encoder for extracting individual robot perceptual embeddings and an attention-based decoder for fusing collaborator perceptions and making decisions. To improve resilience to corrupted observations, we design a novel plug-and-play reweighting module that down-weights the influence of corrupted inputs by analyzing the consistency of neighborhood points relative to the local structure and assigning smaller weights to points that deviate strongly from the local median. This module can be seamlessly integrated into attention-based collaborative decision-making without requiring additional training. We evaluate our method in high-fidelity simulations, considering perceptual noise and five types of attacks across diverse accident-prone scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing methods by up to 26% and achieves state-of-the-art resilient performance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Resilient Collaborative Decision-Making framework that uses attention-based modules and a plug-and-play reweighting mechanism to counter corrupted inputs and improve reliability in multi-robot systems.
AI.motivation: Collaborative decision-making in multi-robot systems is compromised by perceptual noise and adversarial attacks, while existing solutions either require retraining with specific assumptions or lack practicality.
AI.method: The framework leverages an attention-based encoder-decoder system to process perceptions and incorporate a plug-and-play reweighting module for reducing the impact of corrupted inputs without retraining.
AI.result: Experiments under high-fidelity simulations show the proposed method outperforms existing approaches by up to 26% in robustness against various attacks and noise scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The RCDM framework effectively enhances resilience in collaborative decision-making, offering practical robustness without restrictive assumptions or extensive retraining.

title: Bridging the Catalog-to-Real Gap: Scalable Product Recognition via Multi-Stage Contrastive Learning
summary: Automated product recognition is a cornerstone of modern retail intelligence; however, accurately matching real-world, in-store images against extensive corporate catalogs remains a major scalability bottleneck for large-scale applications. In this work, we address this challenge by reformulating the task as an embedding-based cross-domain retrieval problem rather than a standard closed-set classification task. Specifically, we define the objective as retrieving the most corresponding catalog reference image for a given real-world product query crop from an expansive inventory. To bridge the severe domain gap between pristine studio packshots and noisy in-store queries, we introduce a novel catalog-to-real multi-stage contrastive learning paradigm (Cat2Real). This framework fine-tunes a vision backbone by systematically exploiting both item-level and image-level similarities to drive targeted hard negative mining. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that our paradigm scales seamlessly to unseen products and categories, yielding outstanding zero-shot generalization performance even in the complete absence of real-world training images for novel inventory.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Cat2Real, a multi-stage contrastive learning approach for embedding-based cross-domain retrieval to match in-store images with catalog images, addressing scalability and accuracy in retail product recognition.
AI.motivation: Modern retail intelligence requires accurate product recognition, yet matching in-store images with corporate catalogs is challenging due to scalability bottlenecks and domain gaps between image types.
AI.method: The authors developed Cat2Real, a multi-stage contrastive learning framework that fine-tunes vision models using item-level and image-level similarities, employing targeted hard negative mining.
AI.result: Cat2Real demonstrated exceptional scalability and zero-shot generalization, effectively retrieving corresponding catalog images even without real-world training data for unseen products.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully bridges the domain gap and improves retrieval accuracy for practical retail applications, enabling effective product recognition at scale.

title: Retrieval-Oriented Code Representations in Agentic Bug Localization
summary:
LLM-based agents are increasingly being used to support software development, yet their performance in repository-level tasks depends on retrieving the right code context. Existing studies have explored file-level localization using traditional information retrieval over file paths and raw source code. However, the role of textual code representations in retrieval and localization remains underexplored.
  We study file-level bug localization as a representation-driven retrieval problem. Across the Long Code Arena (LCA) and SWE-bench Verified (SWE) datasets, we compare five code representations: file paths, raw source code, and three LLM-generated textual representations. Our experiments include lexical, semantic, and LLM-based retrieval, followed by LLM-based post-retrieval ranking. We quantify the cost incurred by a representation through the representation footprint. We find that the choice of code representation affects both localization effectiveness and cost. Role-aware summaries outperform file-path representations by up to 40% Hit@5 while requiring a representation footprint 10.4 to 20.9x smaller than raw source code. Combining complementary representation results and ranking retrieved candidates with an LLM provides further gains of up to 31.9% and 42.0%, respectively. Overall, role-aware summaries provide the best cost-effectiveness trade-off, while raw source code offers effectiveness in some settings at a significantly higher cost. A case study with Agentless reveals the utility of our techniques within a well-known pipeline, reaching 94% Hit@6 on file localization (+4.7% against the baseline). Our findings suggest that code representation should be treated as a first-class design choice in agentic localization pipelines, guided by pipeline stage and cost-accuracy requirements.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the role of textual code representations in improving file-level bug localization using LLM-based approaches, achieving better effectiveness-cost trade-offs and showcasing their utility in localization pipelines.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents are increasingly used in software development, but their success in repository-level tasks depends on retrieving accurate code context. Traditional methods focus on file paths and raw source code without fully exploring the role of textual code representations in retrieval.
AI.method: The study compares five code representations, including three LLM-generated ones, across various datasets (LCA and SWE). It evaluates lexical, semantic, and LLM-based retrieval methods, followed by LLM-based ranking. Representation footprints are quantified to gauge cost-effectiveness.
AI.result: Role-aware summaries surpass file-path representations by up to 40% Hit@5 while using significantly smaller representation footprints (10.4x to 20.9x smaller). Combining representations and LLM ranking achieves further accuracy improvements of up to 42%.
AI.conclusion: Role-aware summaries offer the best effectiveness-cost balance for file-level bug localization and validate the use of textual code representations in LLM-based localization pipelines. Code representation should be an important design choice based on specific pipeline needs.

title: EvoCUA-1.5: Online Reinforcement Learning for Multi-turn Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents must solve long-horizon tasks through repeated interaction with partially observable, multimodal desktop environments. Although imitation learning and offline trajectory refinement provide strong priors, static traces cannot cover the causal feedback loop of real computer use: each action changes the screen state, future action space, and recovery options. EvoCUA-1.5 extends self-evolving computer-use agents from offline experience learning to online reinforcement learning, where policies interact with executable sandbox environments and improve from verifiable task outcomes. Online RL in this setting requires more than directly reusing single-turn language-RL recipes. Multi-turn interaction introduces context-managed observations, sparse terminal rewards, variable-length trajectories, and slow environment feedback. EvoCUA-1.5 addresses these challenges with Step-Level Policy Optimization (STEPO), which preserves trajectory-level advantage balance after decomposition into step-level samples; policy-aware filtering and pass-rate calibration over verifiable synthesized tasks; Dynamic Tri-Adaptive Curriculum (DTAC), which combines learnable tasks, difficult positive replay, and controlled infeasible-task exposure; and a fully asynchronous RL infrastructure with staleness control and mini-group batching. Experiments show that these components improve training stability and downstream performance. EvoCUA-1.5 achieves 63.2\% success on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming comparable 32B/35B-scale open-weight baselines and even approaching models with significantly larger parameter counts. Overall, EvoCUA-1.5 provides a practical framework for scaling online RL in multi-turn computer-use agents.

AI.tldr: EvoCUA-1.5 enhances self-evolving computer-use agents from offline learning to online reinforcement learning in sandbox desktop environments.
AI.motivation: To tackle the limitations of static traces in imitation learning for computer-use agents, which fail to address the causal feedback loops inherent to real computer use.
AI.method: Introducing EvoCUA-1.5 with STEPO for step-level policy optimization, DTAC for adaptive curriculum design, and a fully asynchronous RL infrastructure.
AI.result: EvoCUA-1.5 shows improved training stability and achieves a success rate of 63.2% on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming similarly scaled models.
AI.conclusion: EvoCUA-1.5 represents a practical framework for scaling online reinforcement learning in multiturn desktop environments.

title: WSqD: A Horizon-Free Learning Rate Schedule for Large Model Training
summary: Standard learning rate schedules such as cosine annealing are tied to a fixed training horizon, limiting their ability to accommodate post hoc horizon extension. Warmup-stable-decay (WSD) partially addresses this issue by maintaining a long constant-rate phase before a short linear cooldown, allowing training to resume from a pre-decay checkpoint. However, its peak learning rate is still tuned based on the original training horizon and can become suboptimal when training is extended. Motivated by stochastic convex optimization, we propose WSqD (Warmup with Square-root base and linear Decay), a learning rate schedule that replaces WSD's constant stable phase with a shifted inverse-square-root base while retaining the final linear cooldown. In the stochastic convex setting, WSqD provably attains the minimax-optimal $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ last-iterate convergence rate. Importantly, its base learning rate schedule is horizon-independent, and the training horizon is needed only to determine when to begin the final cooldown. Empirically, on language-model pretraining using the SlimPajama corpus, WSqD matches or outperforms carefully tuned WSD and other baselines across multiple training horizons while reusing a single peak learning rate.

AI.tldr: Standard learning rate schedules constrain flexibility, especially for extended training. WSqD, a new schedule, optimizes horizon-independent learning rate behavior for improved outcomes.
AI.motivation: Current learning rate schedules struggle with horizon changes, leading to inefficiencies in extended training scenarios.
AI.method: WSqD introduces a shifted inverse-square-root phase and linear cooldown, achieving horizon-independence and minimizing convergence rates for stochastic optimization.
AI.result: WSqD was empirically validated in language model pretraining, outperforming traditional methods across differing horizons, while using consistent peak learning rates.
AI.conclusion: WSqD improves flexibility and performance in varied training horizons, offering a robust alternative to standard schedules like WSD.

title: Enjoy Your Talk: A Human-Centered Benchmark for Multi-Turn Dialogue with Decoupled User Simulation, Target Modeling, and Judging
summary: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) as multi-turn conversational partners requires probing capabilities that single-turn benchmarks miss: persona consistency, evolving intent tracking, emotional dynamics, and goal completion. We introduce EYT-Bench, a human-centered benchmark built around a three-party decoupled design: a persona-grounded user simulator, a target model that separates intent perception from response generation, and an independent third-party LLM judge with optional multi-judge ensembling. Personas are sampled from public human-curated corpora, Nemotron-Personas-USA and PersonaMem-v2, rather than synthesized, reducing LLM-induced persona bias. EYT-Bench also introduces two trajectory-level metrics: embedding-based intent drift and final-intent completion rate (FICR), inspired by tau-bench. In a 17-target x 200-dialogue evaluation, EYT-Bench reveals four findings: (i) state-of-the-art closed- and open-source models are statistically close on subjective dimensions (empathy / persona / anthropomorphism vary within <= 0.3), but differ by up to 9x on objective intent tracking; (ii) reasoning ("thinking on") sharply improves objective tracking on long-context personas (+0.47-0.50 latent-intent accuracy on Gemma-4) while leaving subjective scores nearly unchanged; (iii) persona format dominates trajectory spread, with FICR saturating above 0.95 on Nemotron-USA but spreading from 0.53 to 0.88 on PersonaMem-v2; and (iv) the warm-up effect is robust on 16/17 models (one outlier, GPT-5.5, reverses the effect), with stable rankings across alpha in [0.05, 0.15]. A cross-judge ablation using deepseek-v4-pro confirms that target rankings and final-intent satisfaction are preserved across judges.

AI.tldr: EYT-Bench introduces a human-centered benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in multi-turn conversations, focusing on nuanced aspects like persona consistency and goal completion, utilizing trajectory-level metrics and human-curated personas.
AI.motivation: To address inadequacies of single-turn benchmarks in evaluating LLM capabilities for multi-turn conversational interactions, focusing on factors like persona consistency, intent tracking, and emotional engagement.
AI.method: A three-party decoupled design involving a persona-grounded user simulator, a target model for perception and response, and an independent LLM judge. Metrics include embedding-based intent drift and final-intent completion rate (FICR), with personas drawn from human-curated corpora.
AI.result: Revealed differences in LLM capabilities, like objective intent tracking disparities between models, improved tracking with reasoning, the impact of persona format on outcomes, and the robustness of a warm-up effect across most models.
AI.conclusion: EYT-Bench effectively benchmarks LLMs for multi-turn conversations by integrating advanced metrics, human-curated personas, and robust evaluation mechanisms, showcasing nuanced differences across models.

title: Quota Marketplace: Dynamic Pricing for Efficient Allocation of ML Training Resources
summary:
The escalating demand for Machine Learning (ML) training resources in recent years has resulted in a substantial gap between the high demand and the available supply. Efficient allocation of these scarce and expensive resources is crucial for organizations to maximize their return on investment. Existing resource allocation mechanisms, like Karma [OSDI'23], are designed to guarantee Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness in settings with dynamic (time-varying) user demands, but fail to preserve these key properties in the presence of demands with heterogeneous values. Given the ubiquity and inevitability of heterogeneity in organizational values of different workloads, effective resource allocation policies must accommodate these variations.
  In this paper, we describe the design, implementation, deployment, and theoretical analysis of Quota Marketplace, a market-based mechanism to efficiently allocate ML training chips (like GPUs), explicitly addressing scenarios with demands of heterogeneous value. We detail the implementation of this mechanism within Google and present metrics that demonstrate its impact. We also discuss many business-critical requirements that the Quota Marketplace handles quite effectively, and document the gains and opportunities it has unlocked. We establish theoretically how this market-based approach achieves the essential properties of Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness by allowing the users to express the value of their workloads and enabling dynamic resource pricing based on supply and demand fluctuations. Ultimately, the market facilitates resource allocation that aligns with organizational priorities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Quota Marketplace, a mechanism to allocate ML training resources efficiently in scenarios with heterogeneous demands. It addresses limitations in prior approaches like Karma and achieves Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness while aligning with organizational priorities.
AI.motivation: Organizations face challenges in efficiently allocating scarce and costly ML training resources to maximize their ROI, especially with dynamic and heterogeneous user demands.
AI.method: Quota Marketplace uses a market-based mechanism, allowing users to express workload values and implementing dynamic pricing based on supply-demand, to ensure efficient resource allocation meeting Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness.
AI.result: Deployed at Google, the mechanism demonstrated its effectiveness in meeting business requirements while maximizing resource allocation efficiency and unlocking new opportunities.
AI.conclusion: Quota Marketplace succeeds in addressing heterogeneity in resource demands while preserving fairness and efficiency, offering a scalable solution aligned with organizational goals.

title: Direct Rotor Thrust Sensing and Feedback Control for Disturbance Rejection of Multirotors Using Load-cells
summary:
Gust disturbances, dynamic vertical inflow and ground effect are key adverse aerodynamic phenomena that induce variations in the forces acting on a multirotor and complicate its flight control. Miniature rotorcraft typically rely on simplified modelling of such effects to compute adjustments in thrust to counteract these forces. In the most basic case, disturbance force estimations are derived from the aircraft's motion and the generated thrust is assumed to exactly match that requested by the controller. However, such systems rely on the aircraft's trajectory to be affected before disturbances can be sensed and compensated. Numerous approaches presented over the last 15-20 years aim to reject external disturbances more quickly, but challenges remain.
  This paper presents a new approach in this category by measuring the instantaneous force of the rotors directly at the point of generation using load-cells and implementing high-speed control to accurately track the desired thrust. Measurements from load-cells were previously considered too noisy to provide meaningful input, but the experiments presented in the paper using purpose-built hardware from low-cost commodity components in single- and dual rotor see-saw models and a flying aircraft demonstrate both the feasibility and the effectiveness of the approach in the presence of complex aerodynamic phenomena.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new method for measuring and controlling rotor forces directly via load-cells to counter aerodynamic disturbances in multirotors.
AI.motivation: Existing multirotor control methods rely on simplified models or detect disturbances only after affecting the aircraft's trajectory. Fast and precise disturbance rejection remains challenging.
AI.method: The authors developed purpose-built hardware using low-cost load-cells to directly measure rotor forces and implemented high-speed thrust control to counteract aerodynamic issues.
AI.result: Experiments with custom-built single- and dual-rotor models, as well as a flying aircraft, show that the proposed method is feasible and effective in mitigating complex aerodynamic phenomena.
AI.conclusion: Direct force measurements via load-cells can improve disturbance compensation and provide a viable solution for enhancing the stability of multirotor systems.

title: Do Transformer Temporal Heads and Post-Pooling Motion Gates Help CorrNet-based CSLR? An Empirical Study
summary: CorrNet is a strong baseline for continuous sign language recognition (CSLR) because it models inter-frame correlations inside the visual encoding stage. In this paper, we study two natural extensions of a reproduced CorrNet system: replacing the BiLSTM temporal head with a Transformer encoder, and injecting motion cues after temporal pooling. We find that the Transformer head does not outperform the BiLSTM baseline, even with a training strategy adjusted for the Transformer, and the two heads have almost the same computational and runtime cost. For the second extension, we design a lightweight module called MotionGate. In our experiments, MotionGate consistently collapses to an identity-like mapping: the gate loses motion selectivity, and the injected residual becomes a weak, non-selective perturbation of the pooled features. These results suggest that explicit motion injection after CorrNet's correlation-based encoding is largely redundant, and that natural-looking architectural extensions in CSLR should be tested carefully instead of being assumed to help.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates two potential improvements to the CorrNet-based CSLR model: switching to Transformer heads and the MotionGate module for motion injection. Neither enhancement leads to better results.
AI.motivation: The study explores natural enhancements to an established CSLR baseline, CorrNet, aiming to better understand its limitations and identify areas for improvement.
AI.method: The authors investigate two modifications: (1) substituting the BiLSTM head with a Transformer encoder and (2) adding a MotionGate module to enhance motion representation after pooling.
AI.result: The experiments show that (1) the Transformer encoder does not outperform the BiLSTM head, and both have comparable costs; (2) MotionGate fails to provide meaningful motion selectivity, rendering motion injection redundant.
AI.conclusion: Careful experimentation is crucial in evaluating architectural changes in CSLR models, as seemingly intuitive modifications may not yield improvements in performance.

title: AgentCheck: A Reproduce-Intervene-Mitigate Workbench for LLM Agents over MCP
summary: Tool-using LLM agents are mostly evaluated assuming all tools work. When a tool times out, returns a week-stale value, or has its description poisoned in deployment, the developer needs a controlled way to reproduce the failure, test a fix, and confirm the fix worked before deployment. We present AgentCheck, an open-source web workbench that turns an MCP server into an intervention surface. AgentCheck runs an agent against its real tools and records every tool response, then re-runs the agent with the response perturbed by a fault (12 types) injector. Matching tool calls are replayed from cache, and later tool calls go live after the agent diverges. This yields a reproduce-intervene-confirm loop: the developer toggles a mitigation, re-runs against the identical fault, and sees if the failure goes away. Scoring has two parts: deterministic pass/fail rules, plus an LLM judge for interpretive labels, validated against human annotations. Across five agents, the best passes 105/120 scenarios and the weakest only 77. The failures are usually silent, confident use of incorrect tool outputs rather than crashes. On the weakest agent, a retry mitigation raises success on timeout error faults from as few as 30% of cases to 100%, whereas stale-data faults remain near 3-4 of 10 regardless of the mitigation. AgentCheck makes these failure modes reproducible, comparable, and verifiable before deployment.

AI.tldr: AgentCheck is an open-source tool designed to help developers reproduce, intervene, and confirm fixes for tool-using LLM agent failures caused by faults such as timeouts, stale outputs, or poisoned descriptions. It provides scoring mechanisms and demonstrates varying levels of fault recovery across different LLM agents.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address the challenge of diagnosing and mitigating faults in tool-using LLM agents, especially when tools fail or provide incorrect outputs during deployment.
AI.method: AgentCheck runs LLM agents against live tools while recording responses, then simulates faults through perturbation and replays matching tool calls for testing. Developers can toggle mitigations and confirm fixes using deterministic rules and LLM judges validated via human annotations.
AI.result: Among the five LLM agents tested, the strongest passed 105/120 scenarios, while the weakest passed only 77. Failures commonly involved confident usage of incorrect tool outputs. Retry mitigations significantly increased timeout error recoveries but had limited effectiveness against stale-data faults.
AI.conclusion: AgentCheck effectively enables controlled testing and validation of fault recovery strategies in LLM agents, ensuring failure modes are reproducible, comparable, and fixable before deployment.

title: From Patterns to Maze Structures: SMT-Based Path Synthesis and 2D/3D Construction
summary: We present a pipeline for constructing maze structures from input patterns such as text or shapes. The central path-synthesis problem is encoded in Satisfiability Modulo Theories as global constraints on adjacency, continuity, and pattern-constrained coverage, allowing each fixed-bound instance to be solved in one call. The resulting path is either a planar, self-avoiding route or a layered traversal with prescribed over--under crossings, and it serves as a scaffold for constructing planar mazes and three-dimensional realizations of woven mazes. This report extends the published Bridges 2026 conference paper with more representative SMT-LIB examples and a fuller account of how synthesized paths become concrete maze constructions in planar and three-dimensional form.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a pipeline to convert input patterns like shapes or text into maze structures using Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT).
AI.motivation: To explore methods of constructing planar and 3D maze structures while ensuring constraints like adjacency, continuity, and pattern-constrained coverage.
AI.method: The approach encodes the path-synthesis problem in SMT as global constraints for solving it in one step. It produces planar or layered routes to construct mazes.
AI.result: A robust method for synthesizing paths that serve as scaffolds for planar and 3D maze constructions is demonstrated. Extended examples and analysis are provided.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system shows that SMT techniques effectively generate complex maze structures from input patterns while respecting defined constraints.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Execution under Dynamic Fees in a Closed-Loop DEX Simulator
summary: Trader-facing dynamic fees are increasingly proposed for automated market makers (AMMs), but historical data do not identify how order flow would respond: trader-facing fees do not vary, trader types are latent, and a replayed tape is not a sequential decision environment. We therefore construct a minimal closed-loop simulator in which the missing signal exists by construction: two constant-product pools repriced by an equilibrium-inspired dynamic-fee rule, fee-sensitive noise flow, and closed-form CEX--AMM arbitrage. Equilibrium is used as a closure principle, not as an object the trader learns. Against a tuned benchmark ladder of schedule, planning, lookahead, and tabular policies, a small DQN is the only evaluated valid policy whose paired improvement over tuned one-step routing excludes zero. On a reserved final block of 1{,}000 seeds with completion forced to 1.0 for every policy, it reduces implementation shortfall under every tested intra-step ordering, by $13.3\bps$ of order notional under the pre-specified agent-last ordering, and the edge is concentrated in, and learned from, dynamic-fee environments: under constant fees the paired difference is indistinguishable from zero. The result is model-conditioned counterfactual evidence about execution control in AMMs, not evidence about historical traders, equilibrium play, or deployable profit.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the impact of dynamic fees on automated market makers (AMMs) using a closed-loop simulator and demonstrates using deep reinforcement learning that dynamic fees can reduce implementation shortfalls.
AI.motivation: Dynamic fees in AMMs are proposed to optimize outcomes, but historical data lacks insights on how order flow responds due to static fee structures and latent factors.
AI.method: The authors built a closed-loop simulator comprising features like equilibrium-driven dynamic fees and CEX–AMM arbitrage. Reinforcement learning policies, particularly DQN, were compared against benchmark policies.
AI.result: A DQN policy successfully reduced implementation shortfalls by 13.3 basis points and showed consistent advantages in dynamic-fee environments compared to constant fees.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic fee mechanisms in AMMs can benefit from model-conditioned strategies like deep reinforcement learning, though results are conditional and not directly deployable to real-world traders or equilibrium scenarios.

title: Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models Using Diversion Decoding
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as a powerful tool for retrieving knowledge through seamless, human-like interactions. Despite their advanced text generation capabilities, LLMs exhibit hallucination tendencies, where they generate factually incorrect statements and fabricate knowledge, undermining their reliability and trustworthiness. Multiple studies have explored methods to evaluate LLM uncertainty and detect hallucinations. However, existing approaches are often probabilistic and computationally expensive, limiting their practical applicability. In this paper, we introduce diversion decoding, a novel method for developing an LLM uncertainty heuristic by actively challenging model-generated responses during the decoding phase. Through diversion decoding, we extract features that capture the LLM's resistance to produce alternative answers and utilize these features to train a machine-learning model to develop a heuristic measure of the LLM's uncertainty. Our experimental results demonstrate that diversion decoding outperforms existing methods with significantly lower computational complexity, making it an efficient and robust solution for evaluating hallucination detection.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes diversion decoding, a method that develops an LLM uncertainty heuristic to detect hallucinations by challenging responses during decoding, achieving robustness with lower computational costs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of existing probabilistic and computationally expensive methods for LLM hallucination detection, improving reliability and trustworthiness.
AI.method: The authors introduce diversion decoding, which challenges model-generated responses during decoding to extract features related to the LLM's uncertainty and trains a machine-learning model to develop an uncertainty heuristic.
AI.result: Experimental results show that diversion decoding outperforms existing methods in hallucination detection while being computationally more efficient.
AI.conclusion: Diversion decoding offers a novel, efficient, and robust approach for detecting hallucinations in LLMs by developing a heuristic for uncertainty.

title: Spectral Origins of the Self-Correction Blind Spot in Autoregressive Generation
summary: Large autoregressive language models exhibit a self-correction blind spot: they reliably fix identical errors when attributed to an external source yet fail to fix the same errors in their own outputs. Prior work has documented this phenomenon empirically, through controlled error injection, error-depth decompositions, RL-based verifier-corrector training, and intrinsic self-verification, but offers no formal model of why generating a token suppresses the ability to detect its error, no quantitative activation condition for correction markers, and no convergence guarantee for reinforcement-learning-based self-correction. We close these gaps with SPARC, a spectral-algebraic theory of self-correction in autoregressive generation. We define the error-propagation operator as the product of per-step attention Jacobians on the residual stream and prove that the blind spot arises if and only if the spectral radius of this operator is at least one. We derive a sharp activation threshold, given as a function of the spectral radius, that a correction marker must exceed, recovering the 89.3\% blind-spot reduction observed with a simple ``Wait'' marker. We further prove that RL-based verifier-corrector training converges at a rate proportional to the squared coupling strength over the square root of the number of samples if and only if the verifier-corrector coupling matrix has spectral norm below one, and that this criterion is invariant across residual-stream autoregressive modalities, unifying text LLMs and autoregressive image and video generation. Experiments across four backbones and a visual autoregressive probe validate every theorem, with spectral predictions matching measured blind-spot rates within 3.2\% RMSE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SPARC, a theoretical framework addressing the self-correction blind spot in autoregressive language models, grounded in spectral-algebraic principles.
AI.motivation: To address the unexamined causes and mechanisms behind the self-correction blind spot in autoregressive models, which limits their ability to detect and fix errors in their own outputs.
AI.method: The authors propose a spectral-algebraic theory, defining the error-propagation operator and deriving mathematical conditions and thresholds for self-correction. They also provide convergence guarantees for reinforcement-learning-based schemes.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple model backbones and modalities affirm the theoretical framework, with predictions aligning closely to observed blind-spot rates.
AI.conclusion: SPARC provides a unifying and verifiable theory for understanding and mitigating self-correction blind spots in autoregressive systems, bridging across text, image, and video generation modalities.

title: TAC-LOCO: Unified Whole-Body Control for Quadrupedal TACtile-Informed LOCO-Manipulation
summary: Dynamic loco-manipulation requires legged robots to coordinate whole-body motion while maintaining stable physical interaction with grasped objects under uncertain external forces. While tactile sensing has been widely studied for robotic manipulation, its role in dynamic whole-body control remains largely unexplored. Existing works without tactile feedback commonly grasp firmly rather than regulate the grasp according to the interaction. We propose TAC-LOCO, a tactile-augmented unified reinforcement learning framework that encodes tactile array observations from compliant grippers into a compact latent representation and joins it with proprioception for unified control of the legs, arm, and gripper. With effective grasp stability reward design, the policy learns to simultaneously track body velocity and end-effector trajectories, moderate grasp force, and prevent object slip under both gradual load changes and sudden release events. We deploy the policy zero-shot on a Unitree Go2 with an Interbotix WidowX 250 arm and tactile gripper, demonstrating dynamic tactile-informed loco-manipulation under varying external interactions, achieving a 47% reduction in grasping force and an object drop rate of less than 1%.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TAC-LOCO, a reinforcement learning framework using tactile sensing to enable legged robots to achieve dynamic loco-manipulation while reducing grasping force and minimizing object drops.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to robotic loco-manipulation often fail to utilize tactile sensing, leading to firm grasping that lacks adaptation during interactions. This paper aims to explore the role of tactile feedback in dynamic whole-body control.
AI.method: The method integrates tactile sensing data into reinforcement learning by encoding tactile information into latent representations. The framework combines this with proprioception for unified control of a robot’s legs, arm, and gripper. Additionally, grasp stability rewards are designed for effective learning.
AI.result: The proposed framework demonstrated successful zero-shot deployment on a Unitree Go2 robot with a WidowX arm and tactile gripper. It showed a 47% reduction in grasping force and less than 1% object drop rate under dynamic interaction conditions.
AI.conclusion: Tactile feedback can significantly improve dynamic loco-manipulation by enabling more adaptable and stable grasp control. The TAC-LOCO framework effectively balances grasp force and interaction stability, enhancing robotic performance in varied environments.

title: Banshee: Target Switch Attacks on Gimbal-Stabilized Visual Tracking Systems via Acoustic Injection
comment: Accepted by the 47th IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy (IEEE S&P 2026). 20 pages. Artifact: https://github.com/U1ltra/Banshee
summary: Gimbal-stabilized visual tracking is critical for modern autonomous systems such as Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). While prior work shows acoustic signals can disturb gimbal internals, the impact of such attacks on real-world applications like UAV tracking and following remains underexplored. Existing demonstrations largely overlook practical challenges for real-world attacks, such as object-motion uncertainty and runtime latency. To bridge this gap, we present Banshee, the first physically realizable attack that induces target switching in UAV visual tracking systems by exploiting acoustic vulnerabilities in gimbal-camera systems. Banshee generates carefully crafted acoustic waveforms that induce optimized adversarial gimbal oscillations, causing directionally biased camera-view drifts that break inter-frame target associations. Consequently, the onboard tracker is driven to switch from the original target to an attacker-selected object with high probability, with occasional target loss. Banshee achieves a 93.6% success rate in simulation across two commercial gimbal systems and five trackers. Real-world benchtop and in-flight black-box attacks against a commercial drone across varied scenarios show an overall 95.5% attack success rate. Our results reveal a practical cross-domain vulnerability between acoustics and vision, highlighting the need for robust designs of gimbal systems and applications. Our code is available at: https://github.com/U1ltra/Banshee.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an acoustic-based attack method, Banshee, that disrupts UAV visual tracking by exploiting gimbal-camera vulnerabilities, achieving high success rates in both simulation and real-world tests.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of practical demonstrations of acoustic attacks on gimbal-stabilized visual tracking systems and their real-world implications, particularly for autonomous UAV applications.
AI.method: Developed and demonstrated Banshee, an acoustic attack that generates adversarial gimbal oscillations to disrupt UAV tracking systems. The method was tested in simulations and real-world settings, exploiting cross-domain vulnerabilities.
AI.result: Banshee achieved a 93.6% success rate in simulations and a 95.5% success rate in real-world scenarios, effectively inducing target switching and occasional loss in UAV visual tracking systems.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals significant vulnerabilities in gimbal-camera systems caused by acoustics, urging the development of more robust designs to mitigate such security threats in autonomous systems.

title: Know Before Fix: QA-Driven Repository Knowledge Acquisition for Software Issue Resolution
summary: LLM-based coding agents have significantly advanced automated software issue resolution, yet they remain highly prone to factual errors caused by insufficient repository understanding. Recent methods attempt to mitigate this limitation through pre-repair repository exploration; however, their fix-driven strategies explore repositories without identifying the agent's knowledge gaps, often yielding imprecise context that fails to bridge the underlying understanding deficit. In this paper, we propose ACQUIRE, a QA-driven framework for software issue resolution. Mirroring how experienced developers first comprehend unfamiliar code before attempting a fix, ACQUIRE explicitly acquires repository knowledge prior to repair. The framework decouples knowledge acquisition from patch generation through two stages: in the first stage, a Questioner and an Answerer collaborate to acquire structured repository knowledge, where the Questioner poses targeted questions and the Answerer produces evidence-grounded answers through autonomous exploration; in the second stage, the Resolver leverages the resulting QA knowledge to generate informed patches. By transforming implicit knowledge gaps into explicit, factually reliable understanding, ACQUIRE accelerates knowledge-intensive repair stages and enables more accurate resolution. Experiments on SWE-bench Verified demonstrate that ACQUIRE consistently outperforms representative pre-repair methods, raising Pass@1 by up to 4.4 percentage points with modest additional cost and time.

AI.tldr: ACQUIRE is a QA-driven framework designed to enhance software issue resolution by explicitly acquiring repository knowledge before fixing.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based coding agents struggle with factual errors due to limited repository understanding, and pre-repair methods fail to address knowledge gaps effectively.
AI.method: ACQUIRE uses a decoupled two-stage process, involving a Questioner (posing questions) and an Answerer (providing evidence-grounded answers) to acquire knowledge, followed by a Resolver for patch generation.
AI.result: Experiments show ACQUIRE improves Pass@1 by up to 4.4 percentage points over traditional pre-repair methods, with low additional cost and time.
AI.conclusion: Transforming implicit knowledge gaps into explicit understanding allows ACQUIRE to consistently outperform other methods in repository exploration and software issue resolution.

title: Length Penalties Make Chain-of-Thought Less Monitorable
summary: Length-penalized reinforcement learning can shorten chain-of-thought reasoning while hiding an influence that drives the model's answer. In our experiments, training with length penalties does not stop misleading hints from steering models, even though the models' chains of thought mention the hint much less often. A token-accuracy evaluation would count these runs as successful because they use fewer reasoning tokens with little accuracy loss; it would miss whether the remaining trace still shows what drove the answer. We train Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-14B variants with different target chain lengths, then evaluate them with biasing-hint interventions on held-out MMLU-Pro-R and four transfer benchmarks. Compression sharply cuts reasoning tokens, preserves most multiple-choice accuracy, and leaves hint influence near baseline. At the strongest target, lower-bound faithfulness falls to 63.1% of baseline for Qwen3-14B and 69.4% for Qwen3-4B; the raw rate at which a monitor catches hint use falls from 69% to 49% and from 60% to 48%. To separate length from content, we randomly delete sentences from uncompressed baseline chains until the remaining text matches the compressed length. Even after this length matching, compressed chains disclose the hint 7-35 percentage points less often than baseline chains that we shorten at random, for both Qwen3 sizes and all five evaluation distributions. Compression therefore does more than shorten reasoning, preferentially removing the cues a monitor needs to see what influenced the answer. Together, these results reveal a compression-monitorability frontier in which cheaper reasoning can preserve answers while making the influences behind them harder to detect.

AI.tldr: The paper explores length-penalized reinforcement learning and its effects on chain-of-thought reasoning, showing that compression reduces token usage and detectability of influences driving model answers without substantially affecting accuracy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to investigate how length-penalized reinforcement learning impacts reasoning transparency and accuracy, specifically whether shorter reasoning chains obscure the cues showing what influenced the model's decisions.
AI.method: The authors trained Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-14B models with varying chain lengths and evaluated them under bias interventions across benchmarks, analyzing how compression affects reasoning tokens, accuracy, and influence detectability.
AI.result: Compression significantly reduces reasoning tokens and maintains accuracy but diminishes the detectability of biases influencing the model's answers. Compressed chains disclose cues 7-35% less often compared to randomly shortened baseline chains.
AI.conclusion: While length-penalized reinforcement learning achieves efficient reasoning processes, it compromises transparency by making the influences behind decisions harder to monitor, posing challenges for interpretability.

title: Actor-Critic Learning for Extended Mean Field Control with Deterministic Policies
summary:
This paper develops a model-free reinforcement learning framework for continuous--time extended mean field control problems, where both the dynamics and reward may depend on the joint distribution of states and controls. We adopt deterministic feedback policies, under which the state--action distribution is induced directly as a push--forward of the state law. This avoids optimization over stochastic kernels and bypasses key limitations of existing approaches in extended mean field settings.
  We first establish a model--free sensitivity formula for parameterized McKean--Vlasov dynamics and use it to derive a deterministic policy gradient formula expressed through an advantage--rate function on the Wasserstein space. We then refine this formula by introducing local value and advantage--rate representations that depend on the state, action, and joint state--action distribution, yielding a policy gradient that includes both action derivatives and measure--derivative terms with respect to the control distribution. These characterizations lead to a martingale--based learning principle and motivate a continuous--time deep deterministic policy gradient algorithm combining particle approximations, measure--dependent neural networks, temporal--difference learning, and exploration in either action or parameter space.
  Numerical experiments on stochastic Cucker--Smale consensus control and optimal liquidation with trade crowding demonstrate the efficiency, stability, and robustness of the proposed method, including problems with explicit dependence on the control distribution.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a model-free reinforcement learning framework for continuous-time extended mean field control problems that avoids stochastic kernel optimization by using deterministic feedback policies.
AI.motivation: Address limitations in existing extended mean field settings by proposing a framework to handle dynamics and rewards influenced by joint state-action distributions.
AI.method: Develop policy gradient formulas using McKean-Vlasov dynamics, measure-derivatives, and introduce a continuous-time deterministic deep reinforcement learning algorithm.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate the method's efficiency, stability, and robustness for tasks like consensus control and optimal liquidation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively tackles control problems with explicit control distribution dependencies, overcoming limitations of prior approaches.

title: When Reasoning Hurts Legal Drafting: The Verbalization Bottleneck in Patent Claim Generation
comment: Accepted to AI for Law Workshop @ ICML 2026
summary: Patent claim drafting is a challenging legal drafting task that requires technical expertise, precise linguistic control, strict adherence to formal conventions, and the preservation of complex logical relationships among claim elements. While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting has been widely used to improve the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs), recent evidence suggests that its benefits may be limited, or even negative, in highly structured or pattern-sensitive tasks. Therefore, this paper investigates whether CoT prompting benefits patent claim generation. We propose a task-specific CoT method for patent claim generation and evaluate its effectiveness through both automatic metrics and human expert assessment. Our results show that reasoning-enhanced prompting can improve claim quality. Moreover, we demonstrate a counter-intuitive but important empirical finding: implicit CoT, where reasoning is kept internal rather than explicitly verbalized, consistently outperforms explicit CoT. Through systematic analysis, we show that explicit CoT can introduce an unnecessary information bottleneck for claim generation. Verbalized reasoning may compromise the quality of final outputs through three specific mechanisms: abstraction of critical details, disruption of internalized generation patterns, and cascading error propagation. Our findings provide new insights into legal tasks and CoT applications.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how implicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting outperforms explicit CoT in patent claim drafting, showcasing limitations of verbalized reasoning.
AI.motivation: Legal drafting for patent claims necessitates precision, logical coherence, and adherence to strict conventions which can push the boundaries of reasoning capabilities in large language models.
AI.method: Introduced task-specific CoT prompting tailored for patent claims and used automatic metrics and human expert reviews for evaluation.
AI.result: Implicit CoT (reasoning internalized in LLMs) works better for patent claim generation, avoiding issues tied to explicit reasoning, such as error propagation and loss of detail.
AI.conclusion: Implicit reasoning should be prioritized over explicit prompting in structured tasks; verbalization can hinder LLM performance in legal contexts.

title: RUBRIC: Realism--Utility Balanced Ranking for Imbalanced Classification
summary:
Class imbalance poses a fundamental challenge in risk-sensitive applications such as fraud detection and medical diagnosis, where minority-class samples are scarce yet critical for accurate classification. Existing oversampling methods generate synthetic samples to rebalance class distributions; however, they often produce large numbers of low-quality candidates that distort decision boundaries or introduce artifacts, leading to overfitting and degraded generalization.
  In this work, we introduce RUBRIC, a generator-agnostic filtering framework that formulates synthetic sample selection as a quality-over-quantity optimization problem. RUBRIC ranks candidates using a realism-utility trade-off: realism is quantified by a learned discriminator that distinguishes real samples from synthetic samples, while utility captures proximity to the decision boundary through a concave margin-based scoring function. We show that, under mild regularity conditions, the proposed filtering strategy monotonically tightens the generalization bound for margin-based classifiers by jointly reducing distribution shift and suppressing near-negative tail contributions.
  Through extensive experiments on credit-card fraud detection and other imbalanced benchmarks, we demonstrate that RUBRIC improves F1-macro and recall while maintaining comparable ROC-AUC across several generators. We also provide explicit lambda-sensitivity analysis to show how users can recover AUPRC when ranking quality is prioritized.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses class imbalance in critical applications by introducing RUBRIC, a framework that filters synthetic data to ensure quality and improve performance metrics like F1-macro and recall.
AI.motivation: There is a need to address the issue of class imbalance in applications like fraud detection and medical diagnosis, where existing oversampling methods create low-quality data that harm classification accuracy.
AI.method: The proposed RUBRIC framework ranks synthetic data based on a realism-utility trade-off by using a learned discriminator for realism and a margin-based scoring function for utility.
AI.result: RUBRIC is shown to consistently enhance F1-macro and recall metrics in experiments on credit-card fraud detection and other benchmarks, while keeping ROC-AUC stable.
AI.conclusion: RUBRIC offers a robust mechanism to improve the quality of synthetic data in class-imbalanced scenarios, effectively improving both generalization and performance outcomes.

title: Millimeter Wave Radar: From Synthetic Aperture to Probabilistic Mapping
summary: Robust probabilistic mapping is essential for autonomous robotic systems operating in challenging environments. While traditional sensors fail in adverse conditions such as smoke and fog, millimeter wave (mmWave) radar sensors offer reliable sensing in such conditions. However, creating accurate probabilistic maps from radar data presents significant challenges due to the inherently sparse and noisy characteristics of radio wave measurements and signal processing steps. In an attempt to address these issues, we establish a complete pipeline from raw radar signals to probabilistic occupancy maps, incorporating Synthetic Aperture Radar processing followed by a probabilistic modeling step. We conduct extensive validation across indoor environments, comparing our approach against different signal processing and probabilistic modeling approaches. We also evaluate mapping quality through downstream path planning performance analysis. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of key parameters and antenna array configuration on mapping performance. The experimental results demonstrate both the effectiveness and limitations of SAR-based probabilistic mapping for real-world robotic deployment. To facilitate future research and broader adoption, we contribute an open-source cascaded mmWave radar dataset with an accompanying GPU-accelerated signal processing pipeline available at https://github.com/rpl-cmu/rpm.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a pipeline to convert raw mmWave radar signals into probabilistic maps for robust mapping in adverse environments, validated through indoor experiments and path planning tests.
AI.motivation: Traditional sensors struggle in challenging environments (e.g., smoke, fog), prompting the need for reliable mapping using mmWave radar sensors.
AI.method: The authors present a full pipeline involving Synthetic Aperture Radar processing and probabilistic modeling, validating it through comparative studies and parameter analysis.
AI.result: The approach demonstrated its effectiveness and limitations in indoor environments and real-world scenarios, with results highlighting optimal configurations.
AI.conclusion: A SAR-based probabilistic mapping method is feasible for robotic deployment, and the contribution includes an open-source dataset and processing pipeline for further research.

title: UniPose9D: Universal Category-Agnostic Object Pose Estimation
summary: Object pose estimation is a fundamental problem in 3D vision. Although recent state-of-the-art approaches achieve strong performance, they often overfit to existing benchmarks and exhibit limited generalization to novel categories and unseen scenes. We propose UniPose9D, a category-agnostic foundation model for 9D object pose estimation: given an instance mask/ROI and either an RGB-D observation or an RGB image with predicted depth, the model estimates rotation, translation, and metric size without category labels, CAD models, mean-shape priors, or reference views. Specifically, UniPose9D samples point pairs from the observed object geometry and uses DINOv2 and PointNet features to predict NOCS coordinates for each pair. To improve accuracy, we introduce a point-pair-based RANSAC N-hop Kabsch--Umeyama algorithm with an adaptive threshold. We further employ flow matching to address symmetric ambiguities and construct a large-scale training set by curating and aligning pose annotations from existing public datasets. Experiments across six datasets show that a single unified model can match or surpass specialist methods while generalizing to unseen objects and in-the-wild scenarios. Our code and model are available on https://github.com/qq456cvb/UniPose9D.

AI.tldr: UniPose9D introduces a generalizable approach for 9D object pose estimation that overcomes limitations of prior methods by using a category-agnostic framework and novel techniques like point-pair-based prediction and flow matching.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the lack of generalization in existing object pose estimation models, which struggle with novel categories and unseen scenes.
AI.method: The proposed UniPose9D model uses instance masks/ROIs, RGB-D or RGB images with predicted depth to estimate object pose without relying on category labels or priors. It leverages DINOv2 and PointNet features for NOCS coordinate prediction and includes an enhanced RANSAC algorithm.
AI.result: Experiments across six datasets demonstrate UniPose9D can match or outperform specialized methods while successfully generalizing to unseen objects and wild scenarios.
AI.conclusion: UniPose9D represents a solid step towards robust category-agnostic pose estimation by combining innovative techniques and demonstrating strong generalization capabilities.

title: RepTran: Search-Based Repair of Transformer Models
comment: 12 pages. Accepted to the Research Track of ISSRE 2026
summary: To ensure the overall quality of AI-enabled software, not only traditional software components but also AI components need to be tested and repaired.
