By coupling motor commands with their environment-conditioned effects, EDAR encourages the learned action space to capture interaction semantics rather than merely command-level patterns. Experiments on simulated and real-robot manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that EDAR improves downstream policy learning, especially in long-horizon manipulation. These results highlight the importance of grounding action representations in executable control structure and environment-conditioned visual change.

AI.tldr: EDAR introduces environment-dependent action representations to enhance robotic manipulation, linking motor commands to expected visual consequences and improving long-horizon policy learning.
AI.motivation: To address issues with noisy and redundant control trajectories in robotic manipulation, and emphasize the environment-dependent semantic nature of actions.
AI.method: EDAR couples motor commands with their environment-conditioned visual effects to create action representations that capture interaction semantics.
AI.result: Experimental results show EDAR improves downstream policy learning, particularly in long-horizon robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Grounding action representations in executable control structures and environment-conditioned effects is crucial for more effective robotic manipulation.

title: Anomalous Frame Detection by Grouping Frame Similarities between Two Videos Computed by Vision-Language Model to Extract Expert Workers' Unique Actions
summary: Maintenance of critical infrastructures, such as railways and power plants, is essential for operational safety and reliability. However, the declining number of skilled maintenance workers poses a serious challenge to sustaining these operations, highlighting the need to effectively transfer expert know-how to less experienced workers. Although traditional interview-based approaches have been used to elicit maintenance skills, they struggle to capture know-how that experts themselves may not consciously recognize. To address this gap, we proposed a method that detects anomalous frames of candidate actions including know-how by comparing a video of manual-based work with that of expert maintenance workers. In a simulated maintenance experiment involving a distribution board, our method targeted 11 types of actions not described in the manual and achieved a 66.9% extraction rate, marking a 50-percentage-point improvement over conventional techniques. These findings underscore the effectiveness of our approach in revealing hidden maintenance knowledge, thereby contributing to enhanced skill transfer and workforce development in critical infrastructure maintenance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method to detect hidden maintenance know-how by comparing videos of expert and manual-based work, achieving significant improvements over traditional techniques.
AI.motivation: The declining number of skilled maintenance workers and the difficulty in transferring expert knowledge to less experienced workers highlight the need for better know-how elicitation methods.
AI.method: The method involves analyzing video comparisons to identify anomalous actions that convey hidden knowledge, focusing on actions not described in manuals.
AI.result: In a simulated maintenance experiment, the proposed method achieved a 66.9% extraction rate of hidden maintenance actions, surpassing conventional approaches by 50 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively captures tacit maintenance knowledge, offering a promising solution for skill transfer and workforce development in critical infrastructure maintenance.

title: GEIS: A Generation-Evaluation-Improvement Loop of Agent Skills for Long-Form Article Generation
summary: Long-form article generation remains difficult for large language models because it combines long context, long instructions, and long outputs. Existing multi-agent pipelines such as STORM improve information coverage by simulating role-specialized agents, but their capabilities are often entangled in prompts and fixed procedures, making them hard to inspect, reuse, or iteratively improve. This paper presents GEIS (Generation-Evaluation-Improvement loop of agent Skills), a loop of named and declarative skills for Wikipedia-style long-form article generation. Implemented and evaluated in Tasi Harness, GEIS composes skills for article writing, browser-based evidence and image collection, diagram rendering, PDF-aware pairwise evaluation, and rule-level skill improvement. Its core writing skill follows Request, Plan, Draft, Audit, Refine, and Deliver; the pairwise evaluation skill produces structured quality reports; and the improvement skill maps recurrent findings into permanent patches to the writing skill in our 20-topic experiment. We evaluate GEIS on 20 Wikipedia Featured Article topics. Under the same generation backend, GEIS improves over the Tasi Harness default writer by 8.0 points on a 100-point PDF quality rubric and outperforms STORM on the two comparable writing dimensions, structural quality and content quality. In the 20-topic improvement experiment, the patched writing skill raises the average score from 82.90 to 86.95, with 17 out of 20 topics improved and the gain mainly coming from content quality. These results show that long-form generation can be reframed from a fixed workflow into an inspectable, modular, and evaluation-guided improvement loop.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GEIS, a system for long-form article generation, and demonstrates that its modular, evaluation-guided approach improves quality over existing methods like STORM.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address the challenges in long-form article generation, such as handling long context, instructions, and outputs, while tackling the issues of entangled and rigid workflows in multi-agent pipelines like STORM.
AI.method: The authors develop a loop of modular skills named GEIS, implemented in Tasi Harness, to simulate systematic article writing, evidence gathering, and iterative refinement. The skills include writing, evidence collection, diagram rendering, pairwise evaluation, and rule-based improvement.
AI.result: GEIS achieves an 8.0-point quality improvement over the baseline Tasi Harness writer and outperforms STORM in structural and content quality across 20 Wikipedia topics. An iterative improvement process further raises the average score, with the majority of topics showing gains in content quality.
AI.conclusion: The GEIS framework successfully demonstrates that breaking long-form article generation into a modular, inspectable, and improvable process leads to better content and structural quality, suggesting a promising new paradigm for AI-driven article generation.

title: Conditional Optimal Bridge for Riemannian Activation Steering
summary: Activation steering offers a lightweight alternative to fine-tuning for controlling large language models at inference time. While many existing methods implicitly optimize a log-density-ratio objective between desired and undesired activation distributions, they do so heuristically rather than deriving it from a principled optimization problem. Moreover, these methods produce query-independent steering directions that can degrade performance on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs. We introduce \textsc{Cobras} (Conditional Optimal Bridge for Riemannian Activation Steering), which addresses both limitations by casting activation steering as a Schrödinger Bridge on the residual-stream hypersphere. This formulation yields, to our knowledge, the first principled derivation of the log-density-ratio steering objective from a well-posed optimization problem. Solving the bridge via entropic optimal transport and extracting the probability flow ODE recovers the widely used density-ratio gradient as a special case when the Sinkhorn potentials are uniform. Crucially, the Schrödinger potentials are evaluated at the current activation, making the resulting steering direction inherently query-adaptive. Empirically, across four models and three alignment axes (helpfulness, truthfulness, and detoxification), \textsc{Cobras} consistently outperforms prior activation steering baselines while avoiding the OOD degradation commonly observed in existing methods. The code can be found at https://github.com/arshandalili/cobras.

AI.tldr: Introducing "Cobras," a novel method for steering activations in large language models based on Schrödinger Bridge formulations, which improves steering performance without degrading out-of-distribution capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current methods for activation steering lack a solid theoretical foundation and fail to adapt steering directions based on the input query, often leading to suboptimal performance and out-of-distribution degradation.
AI.method: The proposed method, Cobras, formulates activation steering as a Schrödinger Bridge problem on the residual-stream hypersphere, employing entropic optimal transport and probability flow ODEs to derive adaptive steering directions directly from the activation distribution.
AI.result: Cobras outperforms existing activation steering methods across various models and alignment tasks (helpfulness, truthfulness, detoxification) without causing degradation for out-of-distribution inputs.
AI.conclusion: Cobras provides a principled and query-adaptive approach to activation steering, overcoming key limitations of prior methods and achieving state-of-the-art performance.

title: Towards Human-level Dexterous Teleoperation
summary: Humans routinely wield tools, swap grasps, and reposition objects within a single hand, seamlessly orchestrating contact transitions that span translation, reorientation, and finger gaiting. Endowing robot dexterous hands with this level of in-hand dexterity through teleoperation requires precise control of object motion via dynamic hand-object contact, yet current teleoperation systems remain far from this capability. To bridge this gap, we take a major step towards human-level dexterous teleoperation by introducing TeleDexter, a hand-object co-tracking controller that maps operator intent into learned, low-level contact execution. The controller is trained on consecutive co-tracking subgoals derived from human reference motions, utilizing a hybrid reward that couples sparse subgoal objectives with dense tracking rewards to enable learning across diverse interaction modalities rather than frame-wise trajectory imitation. The entire pipeline requires only single-stage RL and, with random action masking and domain randomization, transfers zero-shot to the real robot. We evaluate TeleDexter on seven challenging dexterous teleoperation tasks spanning object reorientation and long-horizon tool use across two dexterous hands, achieving a 75% average success rate where all baselines consistently fail. Furthermore, the collected demonstrations successfully train autonomous policies via behavioral cloning, marking a concrete step towards human-level dexterous teleoperation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TeleDexter, a system enabling dexterous teleoperation of robotic hands. It maps user intent to fine-grained, learned control of hands and objects using reinforcement learning and performs well on challenging manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing teleoperation systems fail to achieve human-level in-hand dexterity that seamlessly transitions between different object manipulation modalities such as translations, reorientations, and finger gaiting.
AI.method: The system, TeleDexter, employs a hand-object co-tracking controller trained using reinforcement learning with hybrid rewards (sparse subgoal and dense tracking). It relies on human motion data, single-stage RL training, domain randomization, and transfers to real robots without additional fine-tuning.
AI.result: TeleDexter demonstrated a 75% success rate on challenging tasks involving object reorientation and complex tool use, outperforming all baseline methods which failed consistently.
AI.conclusion: TeleDexter makes significant progress towards human-level dexterous teleoperation, enabling robots to perform intricate hand-object manipulations. Moreover, its collected demonstrations can help train autonomous manipulation policies effectively.

title: End-to-End Real-Time Drone-Based Person Detection Framework Using Deep Learning
summary: In recent years, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) or drones have gained rapid response in terms of security, search and rescue (SAR), border surveillance, etc. Existing monitoring frameworks often struggle to maintain detection consistency when targets undergo significant scale variations due to altitude changes, leading to critical information gaps. To address this issue, this work proposes an integrated real-time detection pipeline for detecting targets through the wireless live drone video feed. Build upon YOLOv8-nano architecture, extensive flight experiments were conducted to determine the detection performance across multiple flight altitudes. Trained on VisDrone2019 dataset, the results of YOLOv8-nano model achieves 57.4%, 41%, 44.8% and 20.3% in precision, recall, mAP and mAP50:95 respectively. While demonstrating on real environment, this analysis revealed that the algorithm achieves near-total detection reliability at altitudes between 16 and 25 meters with the detection frame rate consistently maintained above 41 FPS and reaching a peak of 50 FPS. However, the goal of this work is to enable real-time person detection from an aerial platform via wireless transmission. This approach effectively addresses the dual challenges of identifying targets at varying scales and ensuring near-to-accurate localization during aerial observation.

AI.tldr: The study introduces a YOLOv8-nano-based real-time detection system for drones, achieving enhanced detection reliability at optimal altitudes with consistent frame rate performance.
AI.motivation: To improve UAV-based detection systems by addressing challenges in detecting targets with significant scale and altitude variations, ensuring consistent and real-time results.
AI.method: The method involves integrating the YOLOv8-nano architecture, training it on the VisDrone2019 dataset, and testing through extensive flight experiments at various altitudes.
AI.result: The YOLOv8-nano model achieved precision, recall, mAP, and mAP50:95 scores of 57.4%, 41%, 44.8%, and 20.3% respectively. It maintained a frame rate of 41-50 FPS and reliable detection performance at 16-25 meter altitudes.
AI.conclusion: This system successfully detects and localizes targets in real-time using a drone's live wireless video feed, performing reliably within optimal altitude ranges to overcome challenges of scale variation.

title: Learning Linear Temporal Specifications from Demonstrations with Uncertainty
comment: This paper has been accepted by the ACC2026 (American Control Conference)
summary: Learning temporal logic specifications from system demonstrations is essential for tasks such as formal verification and controller synthesis, especially in safety-critical domains. Existing approaches typically assume demonstrations are correct or only affected by misclassification errors. In practice, however, system traces are often uncertain or incomplete due to sensor faults, measurement errors, or data loss. We present a framework for learning minimal Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) formulas from demonstrations with uncertainty. Our approach models uncertainty via Hamming distance to generate possible estimates around each observed trace, which are grouped with constraints requiring that at least one trace per group is consistent with the learned formula. Our problem is then reduced to an equivalent Pseudo-Boolean Optimization. We evaluate our method against state-of-the-art LTL learning approaches and show that it recovers specifications that more closely align with ground-truth formulas under uncertainty.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework for learning Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) formulas from uncertain demonstrations, leveraging Hamming distance and transforming the problem into Pseudo-Boolean Optimization.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of learning LTL specifications from uncertain or incomplete system traces, which is critical for tasks like verification and controller synthesis in safety-critical applications.
AI.method: The method uses Hamming distance to model uncertainty in traces and formulates constraints that ensure at least one trace per group aligns with the learned LTL formula, reducing the problem to Pseudo-Boolean Optimization.
AI.result: The proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in recovering specifications that better match ground-truth formulas under uncertain conditions.
AI.conclusion: This framework provides a robust way to learn minimal LTL formulas from uncertain demonstrations, enhancing specification accuracy in scenarios with trace uncertainty.

title: Dzongkha Next Word Prediction System
summary: Dzongkha, being the national language of Bhutan, is a common and widely spoken language in the country. Official documents, scriptures and other literature products are written in Dzongkha in order to retain the cultural value. However, documenting Dzongkha writing is a challenging and time-consuming process, largely due to the complexity of the script, the need for multiple keystrokes per syllable, and the limited availability of efficient typing tools. An immediate system that can predict and display a list of probable words for Dzongkha is the solution for this problem. The project is mainly aimed to make Dzongkha typing as convenient as possible by reducing the number of keystrokes. Our dataset is acquired from DCDD and has a total of 100000 sentences, 1331282 words and 28344 unique words. The data preprocessing was done by removing all the alphanumeric characters, tokenization, generating N-gram sequences and padding. Three models selected for training are LSTM, Bi-LSTM and GRU. The training process included fine-tuning of the model's hyperparameters. GRU being lightweight and able to handle larger datasets performed best with 74.03% accuracy and also solved the problem of overfitting.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a solution for simplifying Dzongkha typing by using predictive text systems. GRU model performs best with 74.03% accuracy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the challenges in typing Dzongkha due to its script complexity, need for multiple keystrokes, and lack of efficient typing tools.
AI.method: The authors used a dataset from DCDD, performed data preprocessing (including alphanumeric removal, tokenization, and padding), and trained three models (LSTM, Bi-LSTM, GRU) after fine-tuning their hyperparameters.
AI.result: GRU achieved the best performance with 74.03% accuracy, addressing dataset size challenges and overfitting issues.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes GRU is the most effective model for predictive Dzongkha typing, simplifying the process and reducing keystroke requirements.

title: LLM-PDESR: Robust PDE Discovery via Subdomain Weighted Residuals and LLM-Guided Symbolic Hypothesis Generation
summary: Discovering governing partial differential equations (PDEs) from noisy observational data is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning. Traditional symbolic regression (SR) methods often struggle to identify accurate equations within vast combinatorial search spaces, largely due to their inability to incorporate essential domain-specific prior knowledge. Furthermore, reliance on pointwise evaluations and discrete finite differences inherently amplifies high-frequency noise, creating deceptive fitness landscapes that derail the optimization process. To resolve these bottlenecks, we propose LLM-PDESR, a framework that integrates the structural hypothesis generation of Large Language Models (LLMs) with a mathematically rigorous evaluation environment. By employing C^4-continuous quintic splines for robust differentiation and subdomain weighted residuals as natural low-pass filters, our approach effectively mitigates the fitness landscape distortion that plagues existing methods. A Pareto-driven feedback loop then enables the LLM to iteratively refine candidate equations, balancing predictive accuracy with structural parsimony. We evaluate LLM-PDESR on 23 canonical PDEs and five structurally novel equations (including a multivariate system) specifically designed to preclude dataset memorization and test true discovery capabilities. Demonstrating real-world applicability, the framework successfully extracts a consistent structural skeleton for an interpretable 1D dynamical surrogate (1D-CACE) directly from noisy ERA5 reanalysis data. Extensive experiments and out-of-distribution testing confirm that LLM-PDESR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methodologies in structural recovery, noise resilience, and the avoidance of spurious complexity and equation bloat.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LLM-PDESR, a framework combining large language models with robust mathematical evaluation methods to discover governing PDEs from noisy data, outperforming existing methodologies in accuracy and noise robustness.
AI.motivation: Discovering governing PDEs from noisy observational data poses challenges due to domain-specific complexity, noise amplification, and optimization issues in traditional methods.
AI.method: The framework integrates LLMs for structural hypothesis generation with robust mathematical evaluation tools like quintic splines and weighted residuals, employing a Pareto-driven feedback loop for iterative refinement.
AI.result: LLM-PDESR accurately recovers structural skeletons of PDEs from noisy datasets, achieving superior performance in structural recovery, noise resilience, and avoiding equation bloat across diverse testing scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LLM-PDESR demonstrates significant promise in addressing shortcomings of existing SR methods, offering a reliable approach for noisy PDE discovery with real-world applicability validated through experiments.

title: See like a Robot: Robot-Centric Pointmaps for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models predict robot actions from visual observations and language instructions. These actions are defined in the robot's own 3D coordinate frame, yet most VLAs observe the scene in the camera frame, creating a frame mismatch between where the scene is observed and where actions are defined. The mismatch is benign under a fixed viewpoint, where the policy can memorize a single observation-to-action mapping, but grows harder as large-scale datasets aggregate demonstrations across diverse camera setups and the policy must generalize this mapping across viewpoints. We address this mismatch with robot-centric pointmaps, images whose pixels store the 3D coordinates of scene points in the robot frame. Pointmaps provide robot-frame 3D geometry while preserving the dense H x W grid expected by pretrained 2D VLAs, so they integrate into existing VLAs with minimal architectural change. On RoboCasa, pointmaps improve both pi0.5 and SmolVLA and outperform representative camera-viewpoint and 3D-aware baselines. In real-robot experiments, their advantage over an RGB-only policy widens when the camera is moved to a placement unseen during training.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the issue of frame mismatches in vision-language-action (VLA) models by introducing robot-centric pointmaps to align observations and actions, enhancing performance and generalization.
AI.motivation: The research aims to resolve the frame mismatch problem between the camera's viewpoint and the robot's action-defined frame in VLAs, especially critical as datasets grow to include diverse camera setups and require viewpoint generalization.
AI.method: The authors propose robot-centric pointmaps, which map 3D coordinates of scene points in the robot's frame while preserving the dense image grid structure, allowing integration with VLAs without significant architectural modifications.
AI.result: Robot-centric pointmaps improve performance on both simulated benchmarks (e.g., RoboCasa) and real-robot experiments, especially when camera placements are diverse and unseen during training.
AI.conclusion: Pointmaps effectively bridge the frame mismatch in VLAs, enabling better generalization and reliability, particularly in scenarios with varying camera viewpoints.

title: WasteAssistant: Regulation-Guided Visual Question Answering Framework for Intelligent Waste Segregation and Sustainable Managemen
summary: Efficient waste segregation is critical for sustainable urban management and environmental governance. Existing automated systems are limited by single-modality visual processing, insufficient contextual understanding, and weak regulatory alignment. To address these issues, we propose a language-guided vision-AI framework that integrates vision-language models and multimodal large language models for joint visual-linguistic reasoning. This framework implements a visual question answering paradigm aligned with India's Solid Waste Management Rules 2016. We construct a new WasteVQA dataset with 13,500 question-answer pairs across 21 waste categories. Experiments show that the BLIP-based model achieves a BLEU score of 0.8291 and a BERTScore of 0.9273, outperforming traditional CNN-based methods. This work improves source-level segregation accuracy, ensures regulatory compliance, and supports scalable deployment for municipal and citizen-facing waste management, promoting multimodal AI in sustainable urban infrastructure. The source code and dataset are available at: https://github.com/Khushkataruka/WasteAssistant

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a language-guided vision-AI framework for waste segregation, integrating vision-language and multimodal large language models.
AI.motivation: Existing systems for waste segregation lack multimodal visual and linguistic reasoning and fail to align with regulatory requirements, necessitating a more robust solution.
AI.method: The authors developed a visual question answering framework using vision-language models and created the WasteVQA dataset, containing 13,500 question-answer pairs focused on waste categories.
AI.result: BLIP-based models demonstrated superior performance with BLEU scores of 0.8291 and BERTScore of 0.9273, outperforming conventional CNN-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The framework improves segregation accuracy, aligns with regulations, and is scalable for municipal and citizen-facing applications, advancing multimodal AI in urban sustainability.

title: SVR-R1: Bootstrapping Multi-modal Reasoning with Self-verification in Reinforcement Learning
summary: We introduce Self-Verified Reasoner (SVR-R1), a multi-turn RL framework that turns a model's own verification into a learning signal for multimodal reasoning. For each query, the model proposes an answer using the same weights, and issues a binary self-verdict (Yes/No). A 'No' triggers a second-chance rethink; a 'Yes,' or a turn cap, finalizes the output for computing the outcome-based reward. SVR-R1 is implemented with GRPO and an asynchronous multi-turn rollout framework and needs no external supervision or auxiliary critics. We evaluate SVR-R1 on vision-language reasoning benchmarks and show that it improves accuracy by a large margin over strong standard GRPO baselines. Training dynamics show decreasing reliance on verification-fewer verification turns, yet higher test accuracy-indicating that the gap between verification and generation narrows as the policy internalizes self-correction and chooses the most confident answer via our framework. SVR-R1 bridges the less explored intersection of inference-time self-refinement and RL training for VLMs, offering a simple yet effective recipe for bootstrapping multimodal reasoning. We will open-source \textbf{SVR-R1} to facilitate future research in VLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Self-Verified Reasoner (SVR-R1), a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for multimodal reasoning that uses a model's own verification as a learning signal.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in multimodal reasoning by integrating self-verification as a mechanism for iterative refinement without needing external supervision.
AI.method: The proposed SVR-R1 uses a binary self-verdict system (Yes/No) for each query, incorporates second-chance rethinking, leverages GRPO, and employs asynchronous multi-turn rollouts to improve reasoning performance without external critics.
AI.result: SVR-R1 demonstrates significant accuracy improvements over standard GRPO baselines on vision-language reasoning tasks, while showing reduced reliance on verification over training.
AI.conclusion: SVR-R1 provides a straightforward and effective approach to combining inference-time self-refinement and RL for vision-language models, showing promise for bootstrapping multimodal reasoning and prompting further research in this domain.

title: PaperRouter-Agent: A Content-Grounded LLM Agent for Personalized Hierarchical Paper Routing
summary: Researchers organize the papers they collect into personal folder hierarchies in reference managers, and route each new paper into the folder where it belongs. This task differs from standard hierarchical text classification. A user's folder hierarchy is not a fixed, shared taxonomy but a private and evolving folksonomy whose folder meanings may be topical, shorthand, venue-based, or process-oriented, and are often defined by the papers already stored inside them. We formalize this setting as personalized hierarchical paper routing (PHPR): assigning an incoming paper to folders in a user-specific hierarchy without per-user training. We propose PaperRouter-Agent, a training-free LLM agent that grounds routing decisions in folder members rather than folder names alone. The agent first narrows the candidate hierarchy, retrieves folder-specific evidence, verifies fit by inspecting member papers, and incorporates similarity-gated feedback from past user rejections. A formative study on real personal libraries shows that PaperRouter-Agent raises overall Recall@1 from 0.39 to 0.61 and Recall@3 from 0.57 to 0.83, with the largest gains on organizational folders defined by metadata such as venue or year, where single-shot methods collapses (Recall@1 0.09 to 0.50). On the public LaMP-2 benchmark, the same approach improves accuracy from 44.5% to 51.5% (+9.0 macro-F1) over a single-shot baseline, while remaining low-cost for practical use.

AI.tldr: This study introduces a method, PaperRouter-Agent, for assigning papers to personalized folder hierarchies in reference managers.
AI.motivation: The motivation arises from the unique challenges posed by personalized, evolving folder hierarchies in reference managers, which are distinct from fixed, shared taxonomies.
AI.method: PaperRouter-Agent is a training-free large language model (LLM) agent that grounds its decision-making in folder contents, narrows the scope, retrieves folder-specific evidence, inspects member papers for verification, and uses user feedback on rejections for improvement.
AI.result: PaperRouter-Agent improves paper routing Recall@1 from 0.39 to 0.61 and Recall@3 from 0.57 to 0.83 on personal libraries, and boosts accuracy on the LaMP-2 benchmark from 44.5% to 51.5%, with notable gains in organizational folders.
AI.conclusion: PaperRouter-Agent demonstrates a significant advancement in paper organization within personalized, dynamic hierarchies by leveraging folder content and user feedback, while remaining practical for real-world use.

title: ERR@HRI 3.0 Challenge: Multimodal Detection of Errors and Anticipation in Human-Robot Interactions
summary: As robots become increasingly integrated into human environments, their ability to detect and respond to errors remains critical for maintaining user trust and interaction quality. While recent advances in machine learning have improved error detection capabilities, most approaches are limited to specific contexts, controlled settings, or pre-extracted features, limiting their generalizability and applicability to real-world conditions. To address this challenge, the third edition of the ERR@HRI Challenge (ERR@HRI 3.0) provided researchers with two complementary datasets that enable end-to-end innovation in methods for both detecting and preventing errors in human-robot interaction. The challenge offered raw, non-anonymized video data from naturalistic settings: (1) the Bystander Affect Detection (BAD) dataset, containing webcam recordings of 45 participants' spontaneous reactions to robot and human failure scenarios; and (2) the Bad Idea dataset, featuring 29 participants' anticipatory facial responses while predicting action outcomes before failures occur. Both datasets were collected via crowdsourcing, capturing the inherent variability of real-world conditions. This naturalistic variability, while challenging, provides an authentic testbed for developing robust error detection systems. Participants developed multimodal machine learning models for bystander reaction detection (Track 1) and anticipatory outcome prediction (Track 2), with an optional cross-dataset generalization track (Track 3). Three teams submitted valid models, all of which surpassed our convolutional neural network baselines. This paper describes the datasets, tasks, baselines, and results of ERR@HRI 3.0, and discusses implications for building generalizable, context-aware, and anticipatory error detection systems for human-robot interaction.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses ERR@HRI 3.0, a challenge designed to improve error detection and prevention in human-robot interaction. It utilized two novel, naturalistic datasets to encourage innovation in generalizable methods.
AI.motivation: Robots are increasingly integrated into human environments, necessitating robust and generalized error detection systems to enhance user trust and interaction quality.
AI.method: The ERR@HRI 3.0 challenge provided two naturalistic datasets, collected via crowdsourcing, to help researchers develop multimodal machine learning models for bystander reaction detection, anticipatory outcome prediction, and cross-dataset generalization. Baseline convolutional neural network models were also included for comparison.
AI.result: Three participating teams developed models that outperformed the baseline, advancing both tracks of error detection and anticipatory systems.
AI.conclusion: The challenge demonstrated the potential for naturalistic data to drive advancements in generalizable, context-aware, and anticipatory error detection systems for human-robot interactions.

title: Spectral Consistent Flow for One-step 3D Medical Image Translation
summary: We present Spectral Consistent Flow (SC-Flow), a 3D medical image translation framework with a single function evaluation (1-NFE) in the latent space. This approach reformulates medical image translation as a stochastic Brownian bridge process that directly constructs a mapping between source and target modalities by predicting the support regularized mean velocity field. To mitigate modality entanglement, over-smoothing, and artifacts induced by the implicit low-pass modulation of the latent average velocity, we introduce a Spectral Consistency Corrector that dynamically regularizes the evolution of the power spectral density via learnable frequency-domain gain modulation. This mechanism establishes an explicit bridge between spatial textures and spectral energy flow, enabling the model to recover fine-grained anatomical fidelity while maintaining global structural coherence. Extensive experiments on four datasets demonstrate that SC-Flow delivers significantly more accurate, consistent, and robust performance across various translation scenarios.

AI.tldr: SC-Flow is a 3D medical image translation framework designed to address image modality challenges through spectral consistency and stochastic Brownian bridge modeling.
AI.motivation: A need for improved medical image translation methods addressing issues like modality entanglement, over-smoothing, and artifacts while preserving anatomical fidelity and global coherence.
AI.method: Incorporates a stochastic Brownian bridge process model and introduces a Spectral Consistency Corrector for dynamic spectral regularization during latent space processing.
AI.result: Demonstrated enhanced accuracy, consistency, and robustness across four medical image translation datasets.
AI.conclusion: SC-Flow effectively advances the field of medical image translation by mitigating previous limitations and improving translation quality.

title: From Checker to Forecaster: Code-Owned Evaluation of Model-Generated Strategic Routes Under Delayed Ground Truth
summary: Many evaluations of model outputs rely either on contracts checkable at evaluation time or on feedback that arrives within the operating loop. We study the complementary setting in which ground truth is delayed, censored, or private, so deterministic code cannot check correctness at scoring time and must instead issue a code-owned provisional forecast. RouteCast instantiates this regime for model-generated typed strategic routes: models propose candidate routes and structured factors; point-in-time evidence, reference classes, and deterministic transformations produce a provisional forecast-ranking; later outcomes evaluate the forecast. In a retrospective venture pilot on 21 binary-outcome cases (6 positive, 15 negative), the whole-packet RouteCast score showed preliminary retrospective discrimination (AUC 0.756, 95% CI [0.471,0.980]), while a blind LLM judge reached AUC 0.678 [0.419,0.897] and an identity-exposed LLM judge reached AUC 0.761 [0.515,0.944], consistent with recognition- or outcome-related leakage risk. A preregistered decomposition ablation on the same binary subset found that converting the identical inputs into typed staged routes was indistinguishable from the whole-packet score (Delta AUC = -0.144, 95% CI [-0.471,0.176]) and from a deterministic heuristic (Delta AUC = -0.089, 95% CI [-0.412,0.278]). The pilot establishes an auditable feasibility result and exposes failure modes; it does not establish prospective calibration, causal decision improvement, route-decomposition advantage, or cross-domain validity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RouteCast, a provisional forecasting system for evaluating model-generated strategic routes when ground truth is delayed, censored, or private. It demonstrates preliminary feasibility but does not confirm broader applicability.
AI.motivation: To address scenarios where ground truth data for model evaluation is unavailable at scoring time due to its delayed, private, or censored nature, necessitating a provisional forecasting system.
AI.method: Using RouteCast, models generate candidate routes and factors, which are ranked via evidence, reference classes, and deterministic transformations for provisional forecasting. Retrospective evaluations are then conducted to validate the method.
AI.result: RouteCast demonstrated preliminary discrimination with an AUC of 0.756, while blind and identity-exposed evaluations by an LLM judge yielded AUCs of 0.678 and 0.761 respectively. A decomposition ablation showed indistinguishable performance between typed staged routes and the whole-packet score.
AI.conclusion: The pilot establishes that auditable feasibility of RouteCast is possible and identifies its failure modes, but it does not confirm prospective calibration, decision improvement, decomposition advantage, or cross-domain applicability.

title: Beyond Benchmarks: Exposing the Hidden Crisis in Bangla Hate Speech Detection
summary: The spread of hate speech (HS) across different social media platforms (SMPs) poses a major concern for online safety and ethical moderation. Automatic detection of HS remains a challenging task, especially in under-resourced languages like Bangla, due to cultural context, implicit expressions, and informal linguistic patterns. This study aimed to expose the crisis of Bangla HS detection systems by diagnosing how and why benchmark-trained models fail to identify implicit, context-dependent HS. Six architectures (FastText + CNN, FastText + LSTM, FastText + BiLSTM, BanglaBERT, BanglaBERT + CNN, and BanglaBERT + BiLSTM) were trained on benchmark datasets (about 75,000 posts) and a merged multi-source dataset (about 120,000 posts), then externally validated on an annotated dataset (about 200 posts) collected from Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube, labeled as HS and non-HS, where HS was further categorized as explicit and implicit. BanglaBERT achieved an F1-score of 91.4% on benchmark datasets but declined to 75.3% on the external set and 63.4% for implicit HS involving sarcasm and emojis. The accuracy of FastText + CNN dropped from 78.0% to 51.2% under similar conditions. Emoji-aware preprocessing improved implicit HS detection by up to 12%, whereas emoji removal caused a notable decline in performance (F1: 0.75 to 0.63). Frequent misclassifications in politically charged or satirical comments revealed over-policing risks. This study not only exposes the generalization crisis due to implicit, culturally embedded, and emoji-laden expressions but also underscores the need for developing adaptive, emoji-aware, and culturally grounded frameworks that ensure ethical moderation while preserving freedom of expression. Findings of this study provide insights for researchers, SMPs, and policymakers to design more context-sensitive HS detection systems for low-resource languages.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies challenges in detecting Bangla hate speech (HS) due to implicit expressions, cultural nuances, and emojis, showing a generalization crisis in existing models and recommending culturally adaptive frameworks.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the inadequacy of current Bangla HS detection systems, which face challenges from implicit, context-dependent expressions, and informal patterns, emphasizing a lack of generalization in available models.
AI.method: Six machine learning architectures (e.g., BanglaBERT, FastText-based models) were trained on benchmark and multi-source datasets, then evaluated using external annotated data to test performance on explicit and implicit HS, with experiments involving emoji-aware preprocessing.
AI.result: BanglaBERT excelled on benchmark datasets with 91.4% F1-score but showed reduced performance on external (75.3%) and implicit HS cases (63.4%). Emoji-aware preprocessing improved results by up to 12%, exposing significant weaknesses in generalizing to culturally nuanced and symbol-laden data.
AI.conclusion: The research highlights the need for context-sensitive, emoji-aware, culturally adaptive HS detection systems, particularly for under-resourced languages like Bangla, balancing accurate moderation and freedom of expression.

title: MemDecay: Region-Aware KV Cache Eviction for Efficient LLM Agent Inference
summary:
Large language model (LLM) agents accumulate heterogeneous context, including system instructions, plans, user turns, retrieved documents, tool outputs, and intermediate reasoning, whose key-value (KV) cache can become a major memory bottleneck. Existing eviction policies generally apply the same attention- or recency-based rule to every token, ignoring semantic structure already available to the agent orchestrator.
  We introduce MemDecay, a training-free, region-aware KV-cache eviction policy. MemDecay assigns tokens region-specific base priorities and decay rates, refreshes retention scores when tokens receive attention, and evicts the lowest-scoring pages under a fixed cache budget while allowing critical regions to be pinned. We also provide a procedure for calibrating decay rates from measured attention lifetimes.
  We evaluate MemDecay at approximately 450 and 1,700 token contexts using Qwen2.5-1.5B and 3B. Across all settings, attention lifetimes differ by an order of magnitude across regions: system-token half-lives range from 148 to 189 decoding steps, compared with 14 to 16 for scratchpad tokens. Pinning preserves system-region facts at full-cache accuracy in every setting, while no baseline preserves more than 13 of 24. Region-aware retention remains effective as context grows, whereas recency-based retention collapses. Accumulated-attention retention performs better on unpinned content, however, and ablations identify attention-score normalization as the main limitation of the current formulation. These results establish semantic prompt structure as a robust signal for KV-cache management while clarifying how it should be combined with attention-based importance.

AI.tldr: MemDecay is a memory management policy for large language models' key-value cache that prioritizes tokens based on their regions and attention lifetimes, outperforming traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Large language model agents suffer from memory bottlenecks due to their accumulation of diverse types of contextual information (e.g., instructions, user inputs, retrieved documents). Existing strategies for managing memory overlook the semantic structure, leading to inefficiencies.
AI.method: MemDecay assigns base priorities and decay rates to tokens based on their semantic regions, refreshes retention scores for attended tokens, and evicts low-priority pages under a fixed cache limit. It includes calibration of decay rates using measured attention lifetimes.
AI.result: The MemDecay method significantly outperforms existing memory eviction policies. System-token attention lifetimes are shown to last much longer than scratchpad tokens. Pinning demonstrated nearly perfect retention for critical system-region information, and MemDecay's retention efficacy remains robust even as context size grows.
AI.conclusion: MemDecay leverages the semantic prompt structure effectively for key-value cache management in large language models. Its region-aware approach provides clear performance advantages over recency-based methods and highlights the potential for combining semantic and attention-based retention strategies.

title: WarpMPC: Large-Batch MPC on GPU via ADMM with Unrolled $LDL^\top$ Factorization
summary: This paper introduces numerical optimizations for maximizing throughput on GPU when solving large batches (10,000 to over 100,000) of sequential quadratic programming (SQP) iterations, where all problems have the same structure. The optimizations are implemented in a toolbox WarpMPC for model-predictive control (MPC) in JAX and Warp. Based on the insight that all MPC problem instances in a batch share the same sparsity in time, cost, and constraints, we propose unrolling sparse linear factorizations and solves, which dominate alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) solver runtime. We avoid memory access bottlenecks and wasting computations via optimized memory layout, padding-reducing segmentation of the unrolled factorization, and dependency level scheduled backsolves, additionally accelerating sensitivity computation. We achieve throughputs of 8,000 to 250,000 SQP iterations per second on nonlinear cartpole, quadrotor, and humanoid robot benchmarks, outperforming baselines by 3$\times$ to 25$\times$. We illustrate practical usefulness by synthesizing a dataset and training a neural network approximation of an MPC in under 4 minutes that stabilizes a nano quadrotor in hardware experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes GPU-based optimizations for large batched sequential quadratic programming (SQP) solutions in model-predictive control (MPC), achieving up to 25× performance boosts.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to efficiently solve large-scale batches of MPC problems that share structure, leveraging GPU parallelism to optimize computation and memory efficiency.
AI.method: The authors implement optimizations in the WarpMPC toolbox using JAX and Warp, focusing on unrolling sparse linear factorizations, optimizing memory layout, and applying segmentation and dependency scheduling during factorization.
AI.result: The approach achieves throughputs of 8,000 to 250,000 SQP iterations/second on benchmarks and outperforms existing methods by 3× to 25×.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods significantly enhance efficiency in MPC problem solving, enabling real-time neural network training and stabilizing hardware in experimental applications.

title: Spectral Heat Flow for Conservative Token Condensation in Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are costly at inference time because they must process long sequences of visual tokens. Existing token pruning methods often degrade under high compression by blindly discarding information, breaking spatial structure or collapsing diversity. We propose SpecFlow, a training-free framework that shifts the paradigm from destructive pruning to conservative condensation, strictly enforcing spatial coverage and statistical conservation to ensure stability. Treating visual tokens as nodes in a $k$NN graph, SpecFlow (i) computes a stable importance field via spectral heat flow to preserve structural coherence, (ii) allocates budgets via adaptive spatial partitioning to guarantee coverage, and (iii) aggregates discarded information into coreset sinks to maintain statistical conservation. The method is plug-and-play, requires no fine-tuning, and is compatible with FlashAttention. Experiments confirm that our SpecFlow outperforms SOTA methods across tasks, VLM architectures, and pruning ratios. Notably, LLaVA-1.5 with SpecFlow retains 95.6\% of original performance despite pruning 88.9\% of visual tokens, offering an exceptional efficiency-accuracy balance. Code is available at https://github.com/Lzy-dot/SpecFlow

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SpecFlow, a training-free framework to improve the efficiency of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) by condensing rather than pruning visual tokens.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiency and degradation issues in Vision-Language Models during inference caused by existing token pruning methods that disrupt spatial structure and diversity, especially at high compression.
AI.method: SpecFlow shifts from destructive pruning to conservative condensation by leveraging spectral heat flow to preserve structural coherence, adaptive spatial partitioning for coverage, and aggregating discarded information using coreset sinks, implemented without requiring fine-tuning.
AI.result: SpecFlow outperforms state-of-the-art methods across tasks, architectures, and pruning ratios, achieving 95.6% of performance while discarding 88.9% of visual tokens in the LLaVA-1.5 model.
AI.conclusion: SpecFlow provides an effective and efficient plug-and-play solution for Vision-Language Models, maintaining high performance even at high token pruning rates while being compatible with FlashAttention.

title: QwenPaw-Data: Bridging Facts, Methodology, and Execution for Autonomous Enterprise Data Analytics
summary: Enterprise data analysis is emerging as a distinct frontier for autonomous agents. Compared with general-purpose interaction and software engineering, it operates in an open, ambiguous, and continuously evolving environment. These characteristics call for a data-agent architecture that treats semantics, methodology, execution, and evolution as first-class system concerns. To this end, we introduce QwenPaw-Data, an agentic data system designed for enterprise intelligent data analysis. QwenPaw-Data consolidates heterogeneous assets from warehouses, dashboards, documents, interaction logs, and historical tasks into reusable, governable, and evolvable analysis assets, then turns natural-language requests into end-to-end analytical workflows spanning data understanding, retrieval, analysis, report generation, and decision support. Its architecture decomposes the problem into three collaborative subsystems: DataBridge provides trustworthy semantic grounding through interconnected metadata, knowledge, and trace graphs; Skill-Hub codifies expert analytical methodology into reusable and verifiable skills; and Host materializes these evidence and method assets into controllable, artifact-centric runtime execution. Across these subsystems, semantics, methods, traces, and feedback are continuously deposited back into the system, forming a self-evolving asset flywheel. Experiments on public benchmarks and real-world industrial BI workloads show that QwenPaw-Data improves both verifiable data access capability and higher-level analytical quality, offering a practical foundation for reliable, traceable, and continuously improving enterprise data agents.

AI.tldr: QwenPaw-Data is an agentic data system for enterprise intelligent data analysis, capable of turning natural-language requests into comprehensive analytical workflows and continuously evolving through feedback.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of enterprise data analysis in an open and evolving environment with ambiguous and heterogeneous data sources.
AI.method: The proposed architecture divides the system into three subsystems: DataBridge for semantic grounding, Skill-Hub for codifying analytical expertise, and Host for runtime execution, with a feedback loop for continuous evolution.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate improved data access capability and analytical quality in public benchmarks and industrial BI workloads.
AI.conclusion: QwenPaw-Data provides a reliable foundation for enterprise data agents, emphasizing traceability, verifiability, and adaptability in intelligent data analysis.

title: Globally Consistent Coloring Schemes for Language Identification
comment: Abstract shortened to fit arxiv limit
summary:
We study how little extra information is needed to make adversarial language learning possible. In Gold's model of language identification in the limit, a learner is given an enumeration of the strings from an unknown language chosen from a countable language collection. The learner guesses the identity of the language over the course of the enumeration, and it succeeds if, eventually, all of its guesses are the correct language. Classical results of Gold and Angluin show that many natural collections cannot be learned in this way. Recent work on trace colorings, motivated by the success of thinking-trace strategies in language learning, overcomes this obstruction by annotating every symbol of every string with a color. We ask whether the learner really needs this whole sequence of colors, or whether one color at the end of each string (a terminal coloring) is enough for language identification.
  We show that just one terminal bit per string is enough for every countable collection of infinite languages. In fact, the colorings can be chosen collection-independently: there is a single assignment of a two-color terminal coloring to every infinite language such that the same preassigned colorings identify every countable subcollection. Thus, in this model, an entire color trace can be compressed to one bit attached to the end of each example.
  Our global construction uses transfinite recursion, and we prove that this kind of nonconstructivity is unavoidable for any bounded number of colors. As a notion of constructivity, we use the formalism of Borel maps (a regularity condition satisfied by natural explicit constructions); we show that no global terminal coloring with a finite number of colors defined by a Borel map can identify all countable subcollections. By contrast, known trace-coloring constructions are Borel when encoded as terminal colorings, but require infinitely many colors.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that language learning models can use a single terminal bit for efficient language identification across countable collections of infinite languages, overcoming prior limitations. Nonconstructive methods are necessary, as finite Borel-based constructs cannot achieve universal identification.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the challenges in language learning models, as prior results by Gold and Angluin showed that many collections of languages cannot be learned in the limit. Recent improvements with trace colorings provided better results but relied on extensive information. The authors aim to investigate whether minimal extra information (e.g., a terminal bit) suffices for language learning.
AI.method: The authors propose and demonstrate that a single terminal bit per string can identify every countable collection of infinite languages. They employ transfinite recursion for global coloring construction and utilize Borel map formalism to analyze constructivity, distinguishing between finite and infinite coloring strategies.
AI.result: The study proves that one-bit terminal colorings can universally identify infinite languages in countable subcollections, but this achievement requires nonconstructive methods like transfinite recursion. Furthermore, finite-coloring Borel maps fail to achieve the same generalization.
AI.conclusion: Minimal terminal information—one bit per string—suffices for adversarial language identification in countable collections. However, achieving this generalization demands nonconstructive approaches, as constructivist Borel solutions are insufficient for universal application.

title: Sharp Concentration Bounds for Bundle-Valued Statistics on Manifolds
summary: Many geometric statistics and manifold learning pipelines routinely produce observations -- such as tangent vectors or local frames -- whose natural home is a varying family of fibers attached to different points of a base manifold, rather than a single shared vector space. Forming empirical averages requires transporting these observations to a common reference fiber, thereby introducing curvature- and holonomy-driven effects that are absent from classical concentration theory. We develop a non-asymptotic concentration theory for such transported empirical means, deriving finite-sample, dimension-free Hoeffding- and Bernstein-type bounds via sharp Hilbert-space inequalities. When shortest paths to the reference point are non-unique, transport becomes path-dependent and introduces a deterministic holonomy bias; we isolate and quantify this bias through bundle curvature and loop geometry, with sharp closed-form formulas for the tangent bundle of a round sphere. The resulting bias-variance decomposition separates the stochastic fluctuation decaying at the classical $n^{-1/2}$ rate in sample size $n$, from a curvature-driven error floor that no amount of additional data can eliminate; minimax lower bounds confirm both terms are unavoidable. We further establish a robust median-of-means estimator achieving optimal rates under heavy tails and the central limit theorem in the reference fiber. Controlled experiments on the sphere validate all theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: This paper develops concentration theory for empirical averages on fibers attached to a manifold, accounting for curvature and holonomy effects, with sharp bounds and validations.
AI.motivation: Classical concentration theory does not address the complexities of observations residing in varying fibers on a manifold, leading to a gap in understanding curvature- and holonomy-driven effects in empirical means.
AI.method: The authors introduce a non-asymptotic concentration theory, deriving Hoeffding- and Bernstein-type bounds, quantifying holonomy bias, developing a robust median-of-means estimator, and obtaining closed-form curvature-based formulas.
AI.result: They analytically decompose bias-variance effects, establish minimax lower bounds for error terms, and validate theoretical outcomes via experiments on geometric structures like spheres.
AI.conclusion: Curvature-driven biases and error floors are intrinsic to the problem, and their framework provides sharp analytical tools and practical solutions for robust geometric statistics.

title: SKooP: Symmetric Koopman Predictions for Faster and More Generalizable Legged Robot Locomotion with Reinforcement Learning
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication at the IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS), Pittsburgh, USA, 2026
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms classically suffer from poor sample efficiency. In robotics, a recent line of work has emerged addressing this problem by encoding physics priors in the learning process. However, most of these approaches are validated on well-defined, low-dimensional benchmark systems rather than high-dimensional robots with complex nonlinear dynamics. In this paper, we introduce \textit{SKooP (Symmetric Koopman Predictions)}, an approach combining the advantages of morphological symmetries with those of a Koopman model learned via autoencoder to enhance policy learning. SKooP learns a Koopman model of the system dynamics alongside the policy. The resulting Koopman predictions are used as privileged observations for the critic, allowing the agent to learn based on smoother, more informative features. We also incorporate group symmetries into the actor, critic, encoder and decoder networks to produce a highly equivariant policy. The SKooP approach is validated via in-depth analysis of the learned Koopman models and symmetric policies to showcase how each of these influences the agent's performance. We also show that the learned policies are transferable to different simulation environments. Our results show that SKooP consistently reduces convergence time and increases the learned reward for multiple challenging bipedal locomotion tasks on a quadruped robot. Project page: https://evelyd.github.io/SymmetricKoopmanPredictions/

AI.tldr: This paper presents SKooP, a novel reinforcement learning approach combining Koopman models with symmetry priors to improve sample efficiency and policy performance, validated on bipedal locomotion tasks for a quadruped robot.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning suffers from poor sample efficiency, particularly in robotics tasks involving high-dimensional, complex nonlinear dynamics. Incorporating physics priors and symmetry principles holds potential to address these limitations, but existing methods are primarily tested on simpler systems.
AI.method: The SKooP framework integrates Koopman models learned via autoencoders with morphological symmetries into reinforcement learning. The Koopman model provides predictive observations for the critic, while symmetries are embedded into key neural network components to ensure equivariance. These innovations collectively enhance learning efficiency and robustness.
AI.result: The proposed SKooP approach demonstrates improved convergence speed and higher rewards across several challenging locomotion tasks using a quadruped robot. Additionally, the learned policies exhibit transferability to different simulation environments, indicating their robustness.
AI.conclusion: SKooP effectively combines dynamical system modeling and symmetry principles to significantly enhance reinforcement learning outcomes in complex robotic tasks, achieving better performance and transferability compared to baseline methods.

title: Answer-Conditioned Chain-of-Thought Distillation for Few-Shot Industrial Vision with Small VLMs
summary: Deploying AI-based visual inspection in manufacturing is hard because requirements change often, new defect types appear, and large labeled datasets are rarely available. We propose answer-conditioned chain-of-thought (CoT) distillation for rapidly adapting small vision-language models (VLMs) to new industrial tasks using minimal labeled data. A frontier VLM receives each training image along with its correct label and generates a justified visual explanation. A 3B-parameter model is then fine-tuned on these reasoning-augmented examples via LoRA. By conditioning on correct answers, we ensure all training reasoning is directed toward the correct conclusion, which is critical because frontier models score as low as 24.1% on our hardest task. We validate on four industrial classification tasks spanning three image modalities using only 18 to 30 labeled images per task. Across 4 seeds per task (32 training runs), our method outperforms direct fine-tuning on all 16 seed-task combinations, with mean improvements of +1.7 to +4.4 percentage points. A controlled equal-budget experiment confirms the improvement comes from reasoning quality, not additional training steps. An unconditioned baseline demonstrates that with out answer-conditioning, wrong reasoning degrades performance by 17.8 percentage points. On weld radiograph classification, the fine-tuned 3B model outperforms GPT-4.1 by 10.0pp using just 24 training images.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method to rapidly adapt small vision-language models to industrial tasks using minimal labeled data by employing answer-conditioned chain-of-thought distillation.
AI.motivation: AI-based visual inspection in manufacturing faces challenges like frequent requirement changes, new defect types, and limited availability of labeled datasets.
AI.method: A frontier vision-language model generates reasoning-augmented training examples by combining training images with justified visual explanations. These examples are used to fine-tune a smaller 3B-parameter model through a technique called LoRA, with correct-answer conditioning to ensure accurate reasoning.
AI.result: The method outperforms direct fine-tuning across 16 different trials, showing an improvement of 1.7 to 4.4 percentage points on average. For weld radiograph classification, it surpasses GPT-4.1 by 10 percentage points using only 24 labeled examples.
AI.conclusion: Answer-conditioned reasoning improves adaptation of vision-language models to industrial tasks with scarce data, delivering significant performance improvements and robustness to incorrect reasoning.

title: AdvNav: Behavior-Guided Black-Box Adversarial Attacks on Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Despite progress in Embodied AI, Vision-and-Language Navigation systems remain vulnerable to adversarial visual disturbances. Most existing methods rely on white-box access to target model gradients, which is often unrealistic for real-world deployed systems and computationally exhaustive due to recursive backpropagation for optimization, limiting their applicability. While previous black-box methods predominantly target single-step, instantaneous decision tasks, they struggle to handle the task complexities and temporal dependencies. This highlights the need for a gradient-free attack method that can effectively disrupt the multistep sequential perception-action loop using only observable inputs and outputs. Therefore, we propose AdvNav, a behavior-guided black-box adversarial attack framework that disturbs an agent's first-person views during navigation. To construct an informative surrogate objective for effective optimization guidance in gradient-free search under the black-box setting, we design a dual-granularity behavior-based feedback, aggregating a trajectory-level performance score representing overall navigation degradation, an action-level reward score considering the potential decision risk, and a deviation indicator, all of which are extracted from the agent's self-output behaviors. This feedback guides a hybrid optimization strategy that heuristically tunes perturbation strength via adaptive updates and evolves noise spatial structure genetically, to iteratively discover the most disruptive noise configuration. Evaluated against Transformer-based HAMT and LLM-based MapGPT with two types of backbones on R2R dataset, AdvNav achieves 49.70/65.96/87.30% Attack Success Rate. The result demonstrates the effectiveness and generality of AdvNav, reveals critical perception vulnerabilities and offers insights for the design of future resilient VLN models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AdvNav, a behavior-guided black-box adversarial attack framework targeting Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) systems by disrupting first-person views without requiring model gradients. It shows high attack effectiveness across models.
AI.motivation: Existing VLN systems are vulnerable to adversarial attacks in challenging real-world conditions. Current methods relying on white-box approaches or black-box methods for simplistic tasks are impractical and inefficient for multistep navigational tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose a black-box framework, AdvNav, which uses dual-granularity behavior-based feedback to guide a hybrid optimization strategy. It adaptively adjusts perturbation and evolves noise configurations to disrupt VLN agents' perception-action loop.
AI.result: AdvNav achieves attack success rates of 49.70%, 65.96%, and 87.30% when tested on VLN systems like HAMT and MapGPT on the R2R dataset, demonstrating its high effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: AdvNav exposes significant perception vulnerabilities in VLN models and provides insights for improving the resilience of such systems against adversarial attacks.

title: Extending LLM Context via Associative Recurrent Memory
summary: Extending the context length of large language models (LLMs) is critical for many real-world applications, yet standard transformers remain constrained by quadratic compute and linear memory scaling. In this work, we investigate the Associative Recurrent Memory Transformer (ARMT) as a practical approach for enabling long-context processing in LLMs, constant memory scaling, and better efficiency. We make three main contributions. First, we construct two domain-specific long-context datasets designed to evaluate realistic workloads, focusing on narrow-domain fine-tuning scenarios. Second, we propose a comprehensive training recipe for ARMT-based context extension, combining continued pre-training, synthetic long-context data generation, curriculum learning, and selective integration of associative memory into chosen model layers. Third, we present an extensive experimental study demonstrating that ARMT-augmented models: (i) process inputs well beyond their original context limits without degrading performance relative to in-limit baselines; (ii) generalize more effectively to out-of-distribution context lengths; and (iii) need 30% less FLOPs while preserving baseline performance within the original context window.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Associative Recurrent Memory Transformer (ARMT) for extending large language models' (LLMs) context length efficiently with reduced memory scaling and computation costs.
AI.motivation: Standard transformers for large language models face challenges with quadratic compute and linear memory scaling, limiting their ability to handle long contexts in real-world applications.
AI.method: The authors develop ARMT, combining continued pre-training, synthetic long-context data generation, curriculum learning, and partial use of associative memory in specific model layers.
AI.result: ARMT-augmented models can process longer contexts without performance degradation, generalize better to out-of-distribution cases, and achieve a 30% reduction in FLOPs while maintaining performance within standard context windows.
AI.conclusion: ARMT provides a practical, efficient method to extend LLMs' context lengths, conserving computational resources while improving generalization and long-context processing.

title: AutoNorm: Understanding Adaptive Normalization in Transformers through Differentiable Gating
summary: Normalization is a critical component for stabilizing Transformer training, yet the choice between static strategies such as Layer Normalization (LN) and adaptive alternatives remains largely task-dependent. In this paper, we investigate a key optimization challenge in differentiable normalization gating. Our experiments show that, on relatively stationary vision tasks, the high gradient variance introduced by Gumbel-Softmax gating can hinder convergence of the routing mechanism, causing learned gates to underperform simple random selection. In contrast, on non-stationary language modeling and classification tasks, sustained gating diversity enables the model to learn more effective layer-wise normalization policies. Motivated by these observations, we propose AutoNorm-S (Stabilized), a training strategy that mitigates optimization instability through a gate-freezing schedule. AutoNorm-S achieves competitive or improved performance across multiple benchmarks, outperforming adaptive normalization baselines on NLP datasets, including PTB and SST-2, while remaining competitive on standard vision benchmarks. These results suggest that decoupling normalization selection from optimization noise provides a practical and principled approach for adaptive normalization in Transformer architectures.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces AutoNorm-S, a gate-freezing strategy for stable adaptive normalization in Transformers, showcasing superior performance on NLP tasks and competitive results on vision tasks.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address optimization challenges in adaptive normalization gating, especially the high gradient variance issue that negatively affects Transformer training on stationary and non-stationary tasks.
AI.method: A gate-freezing schedule, AutoNorm-S, is devised to stabilize training by reducing the optimization noise in normalization gating.
AI.result: AutoNorm-S outperforms other adaptive normalization baselines in NLP datasets such as PTB and SST-2, while achieving competitive results on vision benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling normalization selection from optimization instability provides a robust and effective way to implement adaptive normalization in Transformer models for different tasks.

title: Breaking the 15% Barrier: A Real-World Data-Driven System for Proactive Social Robot Triggered by User Nonverbal Cues
comment: 8 pages, accepted as a conference paper for IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS2026)
summary: Service robots in retail stores increasingly rely on cascaded speech pipelines (STT-LLM-TTS), yet many customer-robot interactions are initiated or guided by nonverbal behaviors such as approaching, waving, pointing, or showing items. This paper studies such cues in a real-world store deployment with a teleoperated humanoid robot and shows that a non-negligible portion of robot turns are triggered by nonverbal behaviors rather than spoken input, revealing a limitation of audio-only dialogue systems. In a 6-day in-the-wild deployment, 15.3\% of robot utterances were initiated by users' nonverbal behaviors rather than spoken input. Based on an analysis of observed customer behaviors, we define a set of frequent, service-relevant nonverbal cues and develop a real-time multi-person, multi-label recognizer that runs online from video. We then propose a dialogue framework that conditions LLM-based utterance generation on recognized nonverbal cue tokens, and optionally leverages a vision-language model when items are shown, enabling proactive robot responses without hand-crafted rules. We evaluate the approach offline on nonverbal-triggered turns and demonstrate an online prototype that reacts to users' nonverbal cues in real time.

AI.tldr: This paper examines how service robots in stores handle customer interactions initiated by nonverbal cues and proposes a framework to support such interactions using vision-based recognition and language models.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation of audio-only dialogue systems in service robots, which fail to respond effectively to nonverbal customer behaviors like approaching, waving, and pointing, common in real-world retail settings.
AI.method: The study analyzed nonverbal cues triggering robot responses during a 6-day real-world deployment, created a real-time nonverbal behavior recognizer from video, and developed a dialogue framework integrating multi-modal inputs and LLMs for proactive responses.
AI.result: Analysis showed 15.3% of robot responses were initiated by nonverbal cues, not speech. The paper successfully developed a system that detects and employs nonverbal cues for initiating LLM-based interactions with customers.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating real-time nonverbal cue recognition into service robot dialogue systems addresses the limitations of audio-only designs and improves proactive and relevant customer interaction, enhancing their practicality in retail stores.

title: HyperBank: A Differentiable Bank of Classical Priors for Few-Shot Spheroid Microscopy Segmentation
comment: Accepted for publication in the IEEE Xplore ICIP 2026 Workshop Proceedings, Computational Optical Microscopy Satellite Workshop of the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP), Tampere, Finland
summary: Few-shot spheroid segmentation must adapt to new cell lines, microscopes, and illumination conditions from only a small set of annotated images. While foundation few-shot segmenters can be accurate, their large opaque backbones make it difficult to understand which visual cues drive success or failure. We study this question with HyperBank, a differentiable bank of classical image-processing operators combining Frangi vesselness, a Sauvola threshold pyramid, structure-tensor responses, gradient magnitude, and Laplacian-of-Gaussian filters. HyperBank is fitted on the annotated support images and evaluated on disjoint held-out images across three independently acquired spheroid datasets. We treat it not as a general replacement for foundation models, but as a compact, interpretable few-shot microscopy pipeline and an analytic-prior probe of which classical cues carry the few-shot signal. The results show that, adapted on the same few annotated support images, a compact bank of analytic priors is competitive with, and on small-cluster, contrast-driven data can outperform, much larger foundation models, while those models remain stronger on externally sourced, texture-dominated spheroids. Leave-one-family-out ablations indicate that the useful few-shot signal is distributed across operator families and strengthened by support-set-tuned morphology.

AI.tldr: HyperBank, a compact and interpretable set of image-processing operators, is a competitive alternative to conventional few-shot spheroid segmentation models, especially in contrast-driven cases.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of adapting few-shot spheroid segmentation to new conditions (e.g., cell lines, microscopes) while maintaining interpretability and understanding which visual cues influence success or failure.
AI.method: The paper introduces HyperBank, a differentiable bank of classical image-processing operators (e.g., Frangi vesselness, structure-tensor responses), fine-tuned on few annotated images and tested on disjoint images from three diverse spheroid datasets.
AI.result: HyperBank was competitive with large foundation models and outperformed them on contrast-driven data, though foundation models performed better on texture-dominated spheroids.
AI.conclusion: Classical image-processing operators, when fine-tuned on few examples, can serve as an interpretable and effective alternative to opaque foundation models for certain few-shot segmentation tasks.

title: Are LLMs Ready for Scientific Discovery? A Capability-Oriented Benchmark for AI Scientists
summary: Existing benchmarks for scientific data analysis evaluate LLMs primarily on code execution or workflow completion, overlooking that scientific analysis serves to support distinct types of scientific claims: hypothesis exploration, statistical inference, mechanistic explanation, each with different assumptions and validity criteria.
