Its performance advantage grows under tighter constraints and across factory sizes, providing robust and adaptive scheduling capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed extensions to the reinforcement learning model effectively address the complications of post-operation time-lags, demonstrating significant improvements in scheduling efficiency and adaptability for prefabricated modular construction.

title: Adaptive Routing for Efficient Diffusion Transformer-Based PNI Prediction
summary: Perineural invasion (PNI) is a critical prognostic factor in cholangiocarcinoma. However, its preoperative prediction from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) remains challenging due to subtle imaging features that extend beyond tumor boundaries into surrounding regions. Conventional convolutional neural networks are limited in capturing long-range spatial dependencies. Transformer-based architectures improve global modeling of volumetric MRI by aggregating spatially distributed contextual cues, yet capturing subtle and noise-sensitive patterns in peritumoral regions remains challenging. Diffusion-based classifiers offer an alternative formulation by leveraging denoising-based class scoring to better capture such subtle patterns. However, these approaches introduce substantial computational overhead due to the combination of transformer-based modeling and iterative denoising processes. To address these challenges, we formulate PNI prediction as a diffusion-based classification problem and implement the denoising network using a transformer-based representation. To improve computational efficiency, we introduce adaptive routing across attention heads, spatial tokens, and MLP width. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves an AUC of 0.731 with 257.57 GFLOPs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a computationally efficient, transformer-based diffusion classifier to predict perineural invasion (PNI) in cholangiocarcinoma using MRI, achieving an AUC of 0.731 with 257.57 GFLOPs.
AI.motivation: Perineural invasion (PNI) is critical for prognosis in cholangiocarcinoma, but its preoperative prediction via MRI is difficult due to subtle imaging features that extend beyond tumor boundaries. Existing methods like convolutional neural networks and transformer-based models face challenges in capturing these subtle patterns effectively.
AI.method: The authors address PNI prediction as a diffusion-based classification problem, implementing a denoising network using a transformer-based architecture. To improve efficiency, they introduce adaptive routing mechanisms across attention heads, spatial tokens, and multi-layer perceptron (MLP) width.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves an AUC of 0.731 while maintaining computational efficiency at 257.57 GFLOPs.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that a diffusion-based classification formulation, paired with a transformer and adaptive routing, improves the prediction of PNI in cholangiocarcinoma from MRI images while optimizing computational work.

title: Training-Free Off-Screen Player Imputation for Broadcast-Based Spatial Football Analytics
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/nowayfootball/offscreen-impute
summary: Spatial football metrics such as pitch control assume access to the positions of all 22 players, yet the most widely available source of positional data -- the broadcast main camera -- shows only 10-16 of them at any moment. We quantify the resulting distortion with an open, reproducible benchmark: a simulated broadcast viewport applied to open full-pitch tracking data (Metrica Sports; three matches, one held out from method development). Ignoring off-screen players -- the visible-only baseline implied whenever a video-based game-state-reconstruction (GSR) pipeline adds no imputation layer -- inflates hidden-zone pitch-control error to 25.1-26.9 percentage points and a mean absolute control-share error of 11.1-13.4 points across the three matches. We then evaluate a ladder of training-free, online imputation baselines that use only observations from the match being analysed. The best overall on these decision-relevant metrics, role-anchored centroid voting (each visible player votes for the full-team centroid by subtracting its running role offset, attenuating the viewport-induced subset bias), roughly halves hidden-zone error (to 12.2-13.8 points) and cuts control-share error to 28-48% of the ignore policy at every viewport width from 36 m to 60 m in all three matches. For occlusions <=9.6 s -- the regime of the closest learned prior work -- it reaches binwise median position errors of 3.3-8.9 m; but 50-57% of hidden-player observations lie beyond that regime. Integrated end-to-end into a broadcast-video GSR pipeline, imputation moves a downstream possession-quality score (Space-Creation Index) by 15.6 and 17.2 points on two real World Cup broadcast windows, flipping the verdict class in one.

AI.tldr: The paper assesses the impact of occluded player data in football broadcasts on spatial metrics and proposes a data imputation method for improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the limitations of widely available broadcast camera data, which excludes some players' positions, leading to distorted spatial football analytics.
AI.method: The paper employs benchmarks using simulated broadcast views and proposes training-free, online imputation methods to estimate occluded players' positions such as role-anchored centroid voting.
AI.result: The imputation method significantly reduces errors in key metrics like pitch control errors and downstream possession-quality scores in real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: Using an imputation method can meaningfully improve football analytics derived from broadcast video, addressing challenges caused by data occlusion and enhancing spatial decision-making metrics.

title: From Global to Factor-Wise Expert Composition in Discrete Diffusion Models
summary: Discrete diffusion models offer a powerful framework for solving complex reasoning tasks, particularly through compositional generation, which combines multiple pre-trained experts to generalize beyond their individual training data. Recent theoretical corrections introduce time-dependent mixing weights to better align composed diffusion dynamics with the intended target. However, these methods are fundamentally limited by working on a per-sample basis, treating each generated state monolithically and ignoring the potential spatial or functional specializations of different experts. In this work, we address this limitation by proposing FactorDiff - a factor-wise composition framework for diffusion models. We posit that samples can be further decomposed into smaller factors, and propose a sampling process that dynamically routes each factor to the most relevant expert. We instantiate this framework with spatial/pixel-level compositions and validate it on the ARC-AGI benchmark, demonstrating that simple factor-specific routing consistently outperforms complex global scalar weighting schemes on tasks that require logical consistency and spatial disentanglement.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces FactorDiff, a framework improving compositional reasoning in discrete diffusion models by decomposing samples into smaller factors, allowing for factor-specific expert routing.
AI.motivation: The study arises from limitations in current compositional diffusion models, which treat generated samples monolithically and fail to exploit spatial or functional specializations of different pre-trained experts.
AI.method: The authors propose FactorDiff, which decomposes samples into factors such as pixels, and introduces a sampling process that dynamically routes each factor to the most relevant expert, improving compositionality.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates superior performance in logical consistency and spatial disentanglement tasks on the ARC-AGI benchmark compared to traditional scalar weighting methods.
AI.conclusion: FactorDiff enhances compositional reasoning by adopting a factor-specific routing mechanism, showcasing its potential to improve upon traditional per-sample global weighting schemes.

title: Single-Teacher View Augmentation: Enhancing Knowledge Distillation with Student-Guided Perturbations
summary: Knowledge distillation (KD) typically relies on the fixed perspective of a single teacher, limiting the diversity of supervisory signals. While multi-teacher distillation addresses this by aggregating knowledge from multiple models, it incurs prohibitive computational and storage costs. To balance efficiency and diversity, recent research has focused on generating virtual views from a single teacher. However, existing methods face a trade-off: random perturbation approaches offer efficiency but lack controlled diversity, while structured augmentation methods require multi-stage training and incur linear parameter growth. We observe that this trade-off stems from a common design choice: using the teacher's strong but static features to generate views. Instead, we propose Shift-Augmented Knowledge Distillation (SAKD), a simple yet effective framework that leverages the student's evolving features as a dynamic condition for perturbation generation. This shift in perspective enables single-stage training while producing adaptive, diverse views through a parameter-free cyclic shift. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet demonstrate that SAKD consistently outperforms random perturbation methods and achieves accuracy on par with two-stage approaches, while using significantly fewer parameters and eliminating pre-training requirements.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Shift-Augmented Knowledge Distillation (SAKD), which uses the student's evolving features to generate dynamic views for improved knowledge distillation efficiency and diversity.
AI.motivation: Addressing the limitations of traditional KD methods that rely on static signals from a single teacher and the computational inefficiencies of multi-teacher frameworks.
AI.method: SAKD generates dynamic supervisory views by leveraging the evolving features of the student model, employing a cyclic shift mechanism for single-stage, parameter-efficient training.
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet show SAKD surpassing random perturbation methods in accuracy and performing comparably to more complex two-stage approaches with fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: SAKD effectively balances diversity and efficiency in KD, eliminating pre-training and multi-stage requirements, making it a practical and robust solution for knowledge distillation.

title: Technical Report on the CVPR 2026@AdvML Workshop Challenge
summary: Vision-language agents (VLAs) are increasingly used to interpret complex driving scenes and support safety-critical reasoning. This report presents the CVPR 2026@AdvML Workshop Challenge on adversarial multimodal attacks against autonomous-driving VLAs. Built on DriveLM-style multi-view visual question answering, the challenge represents each scene with six synchronized camera images and a structured collection of driving-related question-answer pairs. Participants generate adversarial images and suffix-only textual perturbations that induce model responses to deviate from reference answers while preserving image fidelity and limiting textual cost. The competition comprises two phases, with Phase II adding a hidden black-box model to assess transferability. We describe the task design, submission rules, evaluation protocol, and leaderboard results, and then examine five leading submissions for which technical reports were available. Across these reports, several recurring patterns emerge: image-side attacks are favored by the suffix penalty; scene-level, multi-view optimization is more effective than treating views in isolation; QA types and graph structure provide useful priors for allocating attack budget; feature-space objectives can improve black-box transfer; and typographic content embedded in camera images exposes a persistent vulnerability in driving VLAs. These findings provide a practical reference for future robustness evaluation and defense design in multimodal autonomous-driving systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a challenge on adversarial attacks against multimodal vision-language agents (VLAs) in autonomous driving, analyzing effective attack strategies and vulnerabilities.
AI.motivation: To test and improve the robustness of vision-language agents (VLAs) in interpreting driving environments, ensuring their reliability under adversarial conditions.
AI.method: Participants conducted adversarial attacks on multi-view VQA-based driving scenes, using both image and textual perturbations. Two evaluation phases were used, with Phase II introducing a black-box model to test transferability.
AI.result: Key findings indicate that image-side attacks are preferred, multi-view approaches outperform isolated ones, and embedded typography poses a vulnerability. Results highlight effective strategies for robustness evaluation in autonomous-driving VLAs.
AI.conclusion: The challenge provides insights into adversarial vulnerabilities and informs future efforts to enhance the robustness and safety of multimodal driving systems.

title: A Production-Oriented Framework for Evaluation of SFX Generation
comment: 8 pages main paper, 7 pages appendix, Proceedings of the 29th International Conference on Digital Audio Effects (DAFx26)
summary: Industrial sound design requires audio generation systems that not only produce realistic audio, but also preserve the perceptual identity of a reference, support controllable variation, and remain efficient for practical workflows. Existing evaluations are usually tied to text-to-audio (TTA), unconditional, or task-specific settings, limiting assessment for reference-guided sound effects (SFX) variation. To address this gap, we present a production-oriented evaluation framework for structured comparison of heterogeneous audio generation and editing methods. Our framework identifies nine production requirements and explicitly accounts for differences in model capabilities, enabling comparison under a common production objective. A two-stage protocol is introduced: (1) a reference-guided audio-to-audio (ATA) variation task, in which all methods are evaluated under the same ESC-50 SFX adaptation setup, and (2) capability-specific analyses of native operations such as SFX morphing, temporal and energy alignment, inpainting, and targeted editing. This framework combines objective metrics (including FAD, ImageBind-based reference alignment, and diversity across generated variants), together with a human study of perceptual identity preservation and transient diagnosis. Our study reveals complementary strengths and trade-offs across baselines for different production needs. Among the full-generation baselines evaluated under a shared ATA setting, AudioX provides the strongest overall trade-off between reference alignment and diversity while still supporting SFX morphing. Other baselines remain most suitable for specific editing operations. Our framework establishes a structured evaluation and decision protocol for reference-guided SFX variation and provides a practical basis for designing future unified industrial audio generation pipelines. Audio demos are on the accompanying web page.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework for structured evaluation and comparison of audio generation methods focused on industrial sound design, emphasizing reference-guided sound effects (SFX) variation.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations are limited to text-to-audio or task-specific settings and do not provide a comprehensive evaluation framework for reference-guided SFX variation.
AI.method: The authors propose a two-stage protocol: (1) a reference-guided audio-to-audio (ATA) variation task using a shared setup, and (2) capability-specific analyses, with the use of objective metrics and human studies to evaluate performance.
AI.result: The study shows that different methods have complementary strengths. AudioX offers the best balance of reference alignment and diversity under the ATA setting, while other methods excel in specific editing tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work introduces a structured evaluation framework for reference-guided SFX tasks, providing a practical tool for advancing industrial audio generation and aiding in the design of unified audio pipelines.

title: An Exact Instrument for State Usage in Selective State-Space Models, and the Input-Driven Migration It Reveals
summary: Selective state-space models such as Mamba route information through a bank of first-order modes whose input coupling is set by a learned selection mechanism. We give an exact instrument for measuring how a trained model uses these modes. Because the state matrix is diagonal, each channel's output decomposes exactly into per-mode contributions, and a per-(layer, channel, window) Gram tensor yields the exact output error of dropping any subset of modes, offline, at any budget. Validated against the reference implementation to a relative error of $2.3\times10^{-7}$ on the Mamba-1 family where it is exact, the instrument predicts a layer's deployed pruning error to a median relative deviation of $5\times10^{-7}$ over $4{,}464$ configurations, its floor set by the reconstruction. Applying the instrument across the Mamba-1 family (130M--2.8B), the deployed 7B Falcon-Mamba, and Mamba-2, we find that trained models re-allocate their state space with the input: which modes carry the signal migrates across contexts, and at the most affected layers a per-input oracle roughly halves the output error of a fixed mode set. Frozen-signal counterfactuals attribute the migration primarily to the input-dependent write map $B_t$; the timestep usually identified with selectivity carries almost none of it. Input-scheduled mode pruning on this measurement outperforms static, Hankel-based, and layer-adaptive rankings at every scale from 130M to the deployed 7B Falcon-Mamba, and at half the state budget it matches the unpruned model. Because the scheduler reads each window's mode usage from a first pass, this demonstrates realizable headroom; we claim no deployed compute or memory saving.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an exact measurement instrument for state-space models like Mamba to monitor their mode usage and improve pruning strategies.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to understand and optimize how state-space models utilize modes for processing information in a context-sensitive manner.
AI.method: A diagonal state matrix enables per-mode contribution analysis and error estimation using a per-layer, per-channel, per-window Gram tensor. The instrument predicts pruning errors and analyzes mode usage under various configurations.
AI.result: The study shows that trained state-space models dynamically allocate modes depending on input context, reducing errors with input-scheduled pruning. The instrument matches unpruned performance at half state budget and surpasses static pruning strategies.
AI.conclusion: The instrument successfully measures mode re-allocation and pruning effects, providing insights into state-space dynamics and enabling enhanced, context-aware pruning strategies in Mamba models.

title: MonkeyOCRv2: A Visual-Text Foundation Model for Document AI
summary: Mainstream visual encoders are pretrained on natural images and cannot be effectively applied to document images without document-oriented adaptation, as dense text and fine-grained character strokes demand character-level visual perception. We present MonkeyOCRv2, a visual-text pretrained model for document AI. First, we construct MonkeyDoc v2, to our knowledge the largest document-image pretraining corpus, comprising 113 million images spanning 17 languages. Second, we propose a pretraining strategy that jointly learns image-to-text generation and pixel-level document reconstruction: the former aligns visual representations with textual content, while the latter preserves character strokes and layout details. Extensive experiments are conducted on five representative document analysis tasks, including text recognition, formula recognition, text detection, document tampering detection, and overlapping text segmentation. Replacing the original encoders with MonkeyOCRv2 consistently improves performance across all five tasks. Finally, we validate its effectiveness as the vision encoder of multimodal large language models on the more challenging tasks of document parsing and document understanding. Kept frozen and paired with a lightweight language model, it yields a 0.7B document parsing model that sets a new open-source state-of-the-art on MDPBench, a recent benchmark spanning digital-born and photographed documents across 17 languages, surpassing the previous best 3B dots.mocr by 2.8% absolute with a vision encoder roughly 11$\times$ smaller. The frozen encoder also powers a document understanding model that outperforms counterparts built on CLIP, DINO, and SAM across eight benchmarks under identical training settings. These results suggest that document-oriented visual pretraining can serve as a foundation for document intelligence in its own right.

AI.tldr: This paper presents MonkeyOCRv2, a specialized visual-text pretrained model tailored to document images, which improves performance on multiple document AI tasks and sets new state-of-the-art results for efficiency and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Mainstream visual encoders struggle with document images due to a mismatch in visual perception needs, such as handling dense text and fine-grained character strokes. The motivation is to develop a document-specific pretraining approach to address these challenges.
AI.method: The study constructs MonkeyDoc v2, a large-scale document-image pretraining corpus, and introduces a pretraining approach combining image-to-text generation for textual alignment and pixel-level reconstruction for preserving visual details. Experiments span five document analysis tasks.
AI.result: Replacing conventional encoders with MonkeyOCRv2 consistently enhances performance across five tasks. When utilized as a frozen vision encoder, it sets a new state-of-the-art on the MDPBench benchmark with a significantly smaller model footprint.
AI.conclusion: Document-oriented visual pretraining, exemplified by MonkeyOCRv2, shows potential as a foundational approach for advancing document intelligence, demonstrating improved accuracy and higher efficiency over existing models.
